speaker: Unknown
en: As product managers, one of our best sets of skills is curiosity and empathy and trying to understand our users.
zh: 作为产品经理，我们最好的技能之一是好奇心和同理心，并尝试理解我们的用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the moment that we're talking to an executive, we forget those skills and those talents.
zh: 但当我们与高管交谈的那一刻，我们忘记了那些技能和天赋。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's your fault if the leaders didn't buy into your idea.
zh: 如果领导者没有认同你的想法，那是你的错。

speaker: Unknown
en: People completely misunderstand how executives make decisions.
zh: 人们完全误解了高管是如何做决策的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What is going on in the head side?
zh: 他们的脑子里在想什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Describe an executive's calendar as a stroke light going off.
zh: 把高管的日程表描述为闪烁的灯光。

speaker: Unknown
en: You wake up at 8:00 AM, you've already got a huge list of urgent things going on.
zh: 你早上 8 点醒来，已经有一大堆紧急事情要处理。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have not had the time, the energy, the wherewithal to center your problems.
zh: 他们没有时间、精力或心力来关注你的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are their goals?
zh: 他们的目标是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are they trying to do?
zh: 他们想要做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: How are they measured, connect the thing you're pitching them with that success?
zh: 他们是如何被衡量的，把你向他们推销的东西与那个成功联系起来？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's ways for us to ask much more interesting questions of our executives.
zh: 我们有办法向高管提出更有趣的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tell me what the board is pushing you on.
zh: 告诉我董事会正在给你施压什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Execs want to be successful too.
zh: 高管们也希望能成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: They want to be good at their jobs.
zh: 他们想把工作做好。

speaker: Unknown
en: Sometimes you have the best idea and they just don't bite.
zh: 有时候你有最好的主意，但他们就是不吃这一套。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the biggest things you can do to build trust is kill things, deprioritize things.
zh: 建立信任最有效的方法之一就是砍掉东西，降低优先级。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're thinking about how do you be more senior, how do you show up in a way that is in a leadership mindset, you get paid to be a domain expert.
zh: 如果你在思考如何变得更资深，如何以领导者的思维模式出现，你是拿钱来担任领域专家的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Your executive is looking for you to be the deepest person in the room.
zh: 你的高管期望你是房间里对专业了解最深的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Bringing your expertise to bear is absolutely crucial.
zh: 运用你的专业知识绝对至关重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to act like a CPO.
zh: 你必须表现得像 CPO 一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Today, my guest is Jessica Fain, who's been a product leader at Box and Slack and Brightwheel, and now at Webflow.
zh: 今天，我的嘉宾是 Jessica Fain，她曾是 Box、Slack 和 Brightwheel 的产品负责人，现在在 Webflow。

speaker: Unknown
en: And she has gotten very, very uniquely good at the art and science of influence, and in particular, influencing executives.
zh: 她在影响这门艺术和科学方面，特别是在影响高管方面，变得非常、非常擅长。

speaker: Unknown
en: Influence might be the single highest leverage skill for product leaders outside of AI.
zh: 除了 AI 之外，影响力可能是产品负责人杠杆率最高的单一技能。

speaker: Unknown
en: We actually get into how AI is changing the skill of influence.
zh: 我们实际上深入探讨了 AI 如何改变影响这项技能。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've never heard a podcast conversation get deep into the art and science of influence, how to actually change people's minds, and the mistakes that people make when they're trying to influence leaders.
zh: 我从未听到过播客对话深入探讨影响的艺术和科学，如何真正改变人们的想法，以及人们在试图影响领导者时犯的错误。

speaker: Unknown
en: We get very tactical and very specific.
zh: 我们变得非常战术化和具体。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a bunch of stuff in this conversation that I've never heard before or thought about.
zh: 这次对话中有许多我以前从未听过或想过的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am very excited for you to learn from Jessica.
zh: 我非常高兴你能向 Jessica 学习。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into it, don't forget to check out lennysproductpass.com for an incredible set of deals available exclusively to Lenny's newsletter subscribers.
zh: 在我们开始之前，不要忘记查看 lennysproductpass.com，那里有一系列专门提供给 Lenny 的通讯订阅者的独家优惠。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, let's get into it after a short word from our wonderful sponsors.
zh: 现在，在我们优秀的赞助商简短致辞后，让我们开始吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Omni.
zh: 本节目由 Omni 赞助。

speaker: Unknown
en: Many product teams today are in the process of debating how to ship AI analytics.
zh: 如今许多产品团队正在辩论如何交付 AI 分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: The hard part is obvious.
zh: 困难的部分显而易见。

speaker: Unknown
en: Having an LLM guess at SQL in production is a huge mess and just a bad idea.
zh: 在生产环境中让 LLM 猜测 SQL 是一团糟，也是个坏主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: Omni takes a different approach.
zh: Omni 采取了不同的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have a semantic layer built in, so that when you embed their analytics, the AI actually knows your business definitions, not just your raw tables.
zh: 他们内置了一个语义层，所以当你嵌入他们的分析时，AI 实际上知道你的业务定义，而不仅仅是你的原始表。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can test queries, validate the reasoning, and lock down permissions before anything hits production.
zh: 你可以测试查询，验证推理，并在任何内容上线生产之前锁定权限。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you want AI analytics in your product without building the whole stack from scratch, check out omni.co/lenny for a free three-week trial.
zh: 如果你希望在你的产品中加入 AI 分析而不需要从头开始构建整个堆栈，请查看 omni.co/lenny 免费试用三周。

speaker: Unknown
en: Companies like Perplexity, DBT, and Buzzfeed, use Omni to ship analytics their customers can trust.
zh: 像 Perplexity、DBT 和 Buzzfeed 这样的公司使用 Omni 来交付他们客户信任的分析。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's OMNI.co/lenny.
zh: 那就是 OMNI.co/lenny。

speaker: Unknown
en: This episode is brought to you by Lovable.
zh: 本节目由 Lovable 赞助。

speaker: Unknown
en: Not only are they the fastest growing company in history, I use it regularly and I could not recommend it more highly.
zh: 它们不仅是历史上增长最快的公司，我也经常使用它，我不能再强烈地推荐它了。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you've ever had an idea for an app but didn't know where to start, Lovable is for you.
zh: 如果你曾有一个应用程序的想法但不知道从哪里开始，Lovable 适合你。

speaker: Unknown
en: Lovable lets you build working apps and websites by simply chatting with AI.
zh: Lovable 让你只需通过与 AI 聊天就能构建正常工作的应用程序和网站。

speaker: Unknown
en: Then you can customize it at automations and deploy it to a live domain.
zh: 然后你可以在自动化中自定义它并将其部署到实时域名。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's perfect for marketers, spinning up tools, product managers, prototyping new ideas, and founders launching their next business.
zh: 它非常适合营销人员、快速构建工具、产品经理、原型化新想法以及创始人推出他们的下一个业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: Unlike Noco tools, Lovable isn't about static pages.
zh: 与 Noco 工具不同，Lovable 不是关于静态页面的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It builds full apps with real functionality and it's fast.
zh: 它构建具有真实功能的完整应用程序，而且速度很快。

speaker: Unknown
en: What used to take weeks, months, or years you can now do over a weekend.
zh: 过去需要几周、几个月或几年的事情，你现在现在可以在一个周末内完成。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you've been sitting on an idea, now is the time to bring it to life.
zh: 所以如果你一直有一个想法，现在是时候把它变成现实了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Get started for free at lovable.dev.
zh: 在 lovable.dev 免费开始使用。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's lovable.dev.
zh: 那是 lovable.dev。

speaker: Unknown
en: Jessica, thank you so much for being here.
zh: Jessica，非常感谢你来到这里。

speaker: Unknown
en: Welcome to the podcast.
zh: 欢迎来到播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Thanks for having me.
zh: 谢谢你邀请我。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's my pleasure.
zh: 这是我的荣幸。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's set this conversation up.
zh: 让我们开始这次对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to be talking about the skill and the art of influence, and in particular, influencing executives and leaders.
zh: 我们将谈论影响力的技能和艺术，特别是影响高管和领导者。

speaker: Unknown
en: People above you that you want to convince to do things you want them to do.
zh: 那些在你之上的人，你想说服他们做你想让他们做的事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Help us understand why this is worth people's time.
zh: 帮助我们理解为什么这值得人们花时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why is this worth the next hour of someone listening to this?
zh: 为什么这值得某人花下一个小时来听这个？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why is this skill so important to people's careers?
zh: 为什么这项技能对人们的职业生涯如此重要？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think as product builders, there's almost not a skill that's more important.
zh: 我认为作为产品构建者，几乎没有比这更重要的技能了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Influence and building momentum behind great ideas is the way that great products actually get built.
zh: 影响力并在伟大的想法背后建立势头，就是伟大的产品实际被构建出来的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if you don't have that influence, if you don't have the buy-in and the backing of your key stakeholders, of your executives, you can't build great products.
zh: 如果你没有这种影响力，如果你没有获得关键利益相关者和高管的支持与背书，你就无法构建伟大的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that I personally went through is just like... I was in a period of like, I don't need to work on this skill.
zh: 我个人经历过的一段时期是就像……我当时处于一种我不需要修炼这项技能的时期。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm just like, I'm just going to do amazing work.
zh: 我当时就像，我就只是做惊人的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's going to show itself, of everyone's going to recognize it.
zh: 它会自己显现出来的，大家都会认可它的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And everyone run into this wall of like, oh man, all these other people are getting promoted.
zh: 然后大家都碰到了像这样的墙，噢天哪，所有这些其他人都在升职。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe talk about that because it feels like that's a big trap.
zh: 也许可以谈谈这个，因为这似乎是一个很大的陷阱。

speaker: Unknown
en: People run into you.
zh: 人们会撞上它。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think why I wanted to talk to you about this is, when I was an ICPM, I was putting forth ideas.
zh: 我想我想和你谈论这个的原因是，当我是一个 ICPM 的时候，我提出了想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was trying to reflect what I was hearing from my customers.
zh: 我试图反映我从客户那里听到的内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: I was trying to get sort of buy-in and excitement about the work that I thought was really going to move us forward.
zh: 我试图获得我认为真正能推动我们前进的工作的某种支持和兴奋感。

speaker: Unknown
en: And at the time that I'm thinking about, I was a PM at Slack, and some of my ideas got people really excited, got me funding and backing, and just really a sense that we could accomplish something great.
zh: 在我想起的那个时候，我是 Slack 的一名 PM，我的一些想法让人们真的很兴奋，为我获得了资金和支持，并且真的有一种我们可以成就伟大的感觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And some of my ideas and the things that I really, really believed in died on the vine.
zh: 而我的一些想法以及我真的、真的坚信的一些东西就这样夭折了。

speaker: Unknown
en: They went nowhere.
zh: 它们哪里也没去。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was so confused and frustrated, honestly.
zh: 老实说，我当时非常困惑和沮丧。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because I felt like, "Hey, I really get this user base.
zh: 因为我觉得，“嘿，我真的了解这个用户群。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really understand what's going to move the ball forward and I don't understand how these decisions are actually being made behind the scenes."
zh: 我真的了解什么能推动事情发展，我不理解这些决策实际上是如何在幕后做出的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I was eight months pregnant, eight and a half months pregnant, April Underwood, who had just been named CPO of Slack.
zh: 而我当时怀孕八个月了，八个半月了，April Underwood 刚刚被任命为 Slack 的 CPO。

speaker: Unknown
en: She had just come back from maternity leave.
zh: 她刚休完产假回来。

speaker: Unknown
en: We had a two-day overlap and I sort of got up my courage and I said to her, "April, I've admired you for a long time.
zh: 我们有两天的重合时间，我鼓起勇气对她说：“April，我仰慕你很久了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love the way you work.
zh: 我喜欢你的工作方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: I really love the way you think.
zh: 我真的喜欢你的思维方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I really want to understand it better.
zh: 我真的想更好地理解它。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you'd ever consider having a chief of staff, I'd love for you to consider me."
zh: 如果你愿意考虑设一个参谋长，我希望你能考虑我。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And when I came back from that maternity leave, I came into a stint as April's chief of staff and was later chief of staff to Tamar Yehoshua who succeeded April.
zh: 而当我从那次产假回来后，我开始了一段担任 April 参谋长的任期，后来接替 April 的 Tamar Yehoshua 也由我担任参谋长。

speaker: Unknown
en: Who has been on the podcast.
zh: 她曾上过这个播客。

speaker: Unknown
en: Who's been on the podcast, and is now a CPO and head of AI at Atlassian.
zh: 她曾上过这个播客，现在是 Atlassian 的 CPO 和 AI 负责人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tamar, don't be mad if I mess up your title.
zh: Tamar，如果我把你的头衔搞砸了，别生气。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I learned through that process is that people completely misunderstand how executives make decisions, what is going on in the heads, in the calendars, in the incentive structures of executives.
zh: 我通过那个过程学到的是，人们完全误解了高管是如何做决策的，在他们的头脑中、日程表中、激励结构中发生了什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And, instead of really understanding where that exec or stakeholder is coming from, they center themselves, they center their own desires, their own motivations, their own slice of the world.
zh: 而且，而不是真正理解那位高管或利益相关者来自哪里，他们以自己为中心，以自己的欲望为中心，以自己的动机为中心，以自己的世界切片为中心。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they end up just not being as successful both in their career, but also in the types of products that they're building.
zh: 他们最终在职业生涯中以及在他们构建的产品类型中都没有那么成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: What's an example of that when you talk about, people don't understand how product leaders actually make decisions, how they decide what to do, what to listen to, what's an example?
zh: 当你谈到人们不理解产品领导者实际上是如何做决策的，他们如何决定做什么，听什么时，这方面的一个例子是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh my gosh.
zh: 天哪。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the biggest example of this is people don't understand executive calendars.
zh: 我认为这方面最大的例子是人们不理解高管的日程表。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of times on Google Calendar, it's blocked.
zh: 很多时候在 Google 日历上，它是被屏蔽的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't see what they're working on, but I describe an executive's calendar as like a strobe light going off.
zh: 你看不到他们在做什么，但我把高管的日程表描述为就像频闪灯在闪烁。

speaker: Unknown
en: You wake up at 8:00 AM, you've already got a huge list of urgent things going on.
zh: 你早上 8:00 醒来，已经有一大堆紧急的事情在进行。

speaker: Unknown
en: You go from a meeting with finance on a budget, to an interview for another executive, to a people problem, to a legal problem, to a product review.
zh: 你从一个关于预算的财务会议，去参加另一个高管的面试，然后去处理人员问题，再去处理法律问题，再去参加产品评审。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the product manager coming to that product review, or the leader who's trying to make a pitch, thinks, "I've been prepping for this meeting for two weeks, three weeks, maybe six weeks since we last spoke," but the executive coming into that session hasn't thought about you since.
zh: 而来参加那个产品评审的产品经理，或者是试图做推介的领导者，心里想的是，“为了这次会议我已经准备了两周、三周，也许自从我们上次说话以来已经六周了，”但走进那个会议的高管从那以后就没想过你。

speaker: Unknown
en: They may not have gone to the bathroom today, right?
zh: 他们今天可能连上厕所都没时间，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have to understand that they have not had the time, the energy, the wherewithal to center your problems and you have to help them get into that mindset.
zh: 而且你必须明白，他们没有时间、精力或能力去关注你的问题，你必须帮助他们进入那种心态。

speaker: Unknown
en: So this is as simple as, execs are people too, right?
zh: 所以这就很简单，高管也是人，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: They are running around context switching in the most insane ways I've ever seen, and everything that comes across their plate is an emergency.
zh: 他们到处跑，以我见过的最疯狂的方式进行上下文切换，摆在他们面前的每件事都是紧急情况。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one of the biggest tactics I think is so important is, just take 30 seconds at the top of a meeting.
zh: 所以我认为最重要的策略之一就是，在会议开始时花 30 秒钟。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why are we here?
zh: 我们为什么在这里？

speaker: Unknown
en: What happened the last time we talked?
zh: 上次我们谈话时发生了什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why is this important to you?
zh: 这为什么对你很重要？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why is this meaningful?
zh: 这有什么意义？

speaker: Unknown
en: And really remember that they are not thinking just about you.
zh: 并且要真正记住，他们不仅仅是在想你。

speaker: Unknown
en: They are optimizing for a global maximum and not for the local that you're optimizing for.
zh: 他们正在优化全局最大值，而不是你正在优化的局部最大值。

speaker: Unknown
en: Essentially, this is how to help someone in this world that is running around from meeting to meeting without context on most things, maybe hasn't have time to go to the bathroom.
zh: 本质上，这就是如何帮助在这个世界上到处奔波、从一个会议赶到另一个会议、对大多数事情缺乏上下文、也许没时间上厕所的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we help them see what you want them to see, and maybe buy into your vision and suggestions?
zh: 我们如何帮助他们看到你想让他们看到的东西，并让他们认同你的愿景和建议？

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that one of the ways that I phrase this is, how do you get the best out of your exec?
zh: 我认为我表达这一点的方式之一是，如何从你的高管那里得到最好的结果？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you help them be their best selves?
zh: 你如何帮助他们成为最好的自己？

speaker: Unknown
en: We often think about how we show up in a meeting, or the doc that we wrote, or the prototype that we've got ready, but we don't think about how they can be at their best.
zh: 我们经常考虑我们在会议中的表现，或者我们写的文档，或者我们准备好的原型，但我们不考虑他们如何才能处于最佳状态。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we give them the right kind of information in the way that they communicate?
zh: 我们如何以他们沟通的方式给他们提供合适的信息？

speaker: Unknown
en: We as product leaders have to be communication chameleons.
zh: 我们作为产品领导者必须做沟通的变色龙。

speaker: Unknown
en: We have to be speaking their language, like love languages, but appropriate for work.
zh: 我们必须说他们的语言，就像爱的语言，但适合于工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you have to think about, do they really turn a spark with a design, with a customer story, with a dashboard, with an experiment?
zh: 你必须考虑，他们真的会因为设计、客户故事、仪表板或实验而兴奋起来吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: And how do you understand how their best brain, how their best expertise, gets turned on to give you the best of themselves?
zh: 你如何理解他们最好的大脑、他们最好的专业知识是如何被激活，从而给你最好的自己？

speaker: Unknown
en: But part of that is literally just setting up the meeting effectively, so that they can have the context, the wherewithal, the sort of breadth at the beginning of the meeting, to dive into it.
zh: 但其中一部分实际上只是有效地设置会议，以便他们能够在会议开始时拥有上下文、能力和广度，从而投入其中。

speaker: Unknown
en: The other thing I always tell people I managed is, it's your fault if the leaders didn't buy into your idea.
zh: 我总是告诉我管理的人的另一件事是，如果领导者不认同你的想法，那是你的错。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't just be like, "Ah, they just don't see it.
zh: 你不能只是说，“啊，他们就是不明白。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not my fault."
zh: 不是我的错。”

speaker: Unknown
en: They agreed to do something else.
zh: 他们同意做别的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: I always tell them, "That's just you're not able to influence them and convince them that you're right."
zh: 我总是告诉他们，“那只是你无法影响他们并说服你是正确的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Annie Pearl, who was my first PM manager back at Box.
zh: 还有 Annie Pearl，我在 Box 时的第一位 PM 经理。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also former podcast guest.
zh: 也是以前的播客嘉宾。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also former podcast guest, former CPO at Calendly and Glassdoor and now at Microsoft, and Annie's just the best.
zh: 也是以前的播客嘉宾，Calendly 和 Glassdoor 的前 CPO，现在在 Microsoft，Annie 是最棒的。

speaker: Unknown
en: She always said to me, "It's not my fault, but it is my problem."
zh: 她总是对我说，“不是我的错，但是是我的问题。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that that's the vantage point that a product leader has to have, especially if they're trying to put themselves in the shoes of an executive.
zh: 我认为这是产品领导必须有的观点，特别是如果他们试图让自己站在高管的立场上。

speaker: Unknown
en: As product managers, one of our best sets of skills is curiosity, and empathy, and trying to understand our users.
zh: 作为产品经理，我们最好的技能之一是好奇心、同理心，以及试图理解我们的用户。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the moment that we're talking to an executive or to a stakeholder, we forget those skills and those talents, and we begin to think about our ideas, getting that approval, getting to the next meeting, whatever it might be, the incentive that we are driving.
zh: 但是当我们与高管或利益相关者交谈的那一刻，我们忘记了这些技能和天赋，我们开始考虑我们的想法、获得批准、去参加下一个会议，无论它可能是什么，我们正在驱动的激励。

speaker: Unknown
en: We don't think about the curiosity and empathy for what they're thinking about, for what their incentives are, for how their day has been.
zh: 我们不考虑对他们所想的、对他们激励的、对他们一天过得如何的好奇心和同理心。

speaker: Unknown
en: And, if we can take some of those skills of building great products and think about our executive as our key user here, then we can have a much, much more productive conversation.
zh: 而且，如果我们能把构建优秀产品的一些技能拿过来，把我们的高管当作这里的 key user，那么我们就能拥有更有成效的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one of the most disastrous things you can do is going into a meeting just looking for approval for your plan.
zh: 我认为你可以做的最灾难性的事情之一就是带着仅仅寻求计划批准的目的去参加会议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Instead, if what you go in with is, how can I learn?
zh: 相反，如果你带着这样的想法进去：我能学到什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: How can I strengthen this plan?
zh: 我如何加强这个计划？

speaker: Unknown
en: How can I use the domain expertise, the context, the experience that this person brings to the table, and imbue that into my product work?
zh: 我如何利用这个人带来的领域专业知识、上下文和经验，并将其注入到我的产品工作中？

speaker: Unknown
en: Both the executive will like you better, because they will feel like you have actually built product alongside them.
zh: 高管会更喜欢你，因为他们会觉得你实际上是与他们一起构建产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: But, you will also end up with a better product.
zh: 但是，你最终也会得到一个更好的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: I often hear people say to me, "Oh, they just don't get it.
zh: 我经常听到人们对我说，“哦，他们就是不明白。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't see what I see.
zh: 他们看不到我看到的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They're not in my meetings.
zh: 他们没参加我的会议。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't know how hard this is."
zh: 他们不知道这有多难。"

speaker: Unknown
en: Fine.
zh: 好吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you don't respect that person, if you don't believe they know something you don't, you need to quit.
zh: 如果你不尊重那个人，如果你不相信他们知道一些你不知道的事情，你需要退出。

speaker: Unknown
en: You need to go work somewhere else.
zh: 你需要去别的地方工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you respect them, if you think they have something to offer you, that they got to the place that they're at because they learned and they developed a skill that you're trying to gain, awesome.
zh: 但如果你尊重他们，如果你认为他们能给你提供一些东西，他们能达到那个位置是因为他们学到了东西并培养了你正试图获得的技能，那很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: Take the feedback, take the insight, ask questions, and don't just treat it like a rubber stamp, because that's a failure state, and it's a failure state that they don't like.
zh: 接受反馈，接受见解，提出问题，不要只是把它当作橡皮图章，因为那是失败的状态，也是他们不喜欢的失败状态。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want to dive deeper into this stuff, but first, something on my mind is some people might be turned off by this topic in terms of, it may feel icky of just like, "Oh, I don't want to try to be this manipulative person, and deal with politics and influence.
zh: 我想深入探讨这些内容，但首先，我脑子里想的是，有些人可能会被这个话题劝退，就这方面而言，感觉可能很恶心，就像，"噢，我不想试图成为一个善于操纵的人，去搞政治和施加影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: I just want to do awesome work.
zh: 我只想做很棒的工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why do I have to do this?"
zh: 为什么我必须做这个？"

speaker: Unknown
en: Speak to that person that's kind of just feels icky about building this and working on this and being this person.
zh: 对那个觉得建立这些东西、做这些工作、成为这样的人感觉很恶心的人说几句。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that when people frame this as politics, they're completely missing the point.
zh: 我认为当人们把这界定为政治时，他们完全搞错了重点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Politics is manipulating outcomes and people for your own gain.
zh: 政治是为了自己的利益操纵结果和他人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Influence is about increasing the odds that your good ideas survive.
zh: 影响力是增加你的好想法存活下来的几率。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think what happens is people actually let ego and let their own narrative, their own scope of influence, get in the way of empathy.
zh: 我认为发生的事情是，人们实际上让自我，让他们自己的叙事、自己的影响范围，阻碍了共情。

speaker: Unknown
en: So, this isn't politicking, this is learning.
zh: 所以，这不是搞政治，这是学习。

speaker: Unknown
en: If we treat our stakeholder conversations as discovery interviews as a way to strengthen our ideas, then we end up in a much, much better place.
zh: 如果我们把与利益相关者的对话当作发现访谈，作为加强我们想法的一种方式，那么我们最终会处于一个好得多的境地。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if we do that in a genuine and low ego way, I'll give an example here.
zh: 如果我们以一种真诚和低自我的方式这样做，我会在这里举个例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Noah Weiss, who succeeded Tamar Yehoshua, CPO of Slack and also former podcast guest.
zh: Noah Weiss，他接替了 Tamar Yehoshua，Slack 的 CPO，也是前播客嘉宾。

speaker: Unknown
en: Also former podcast guest.
zh: 也是前播客嘉宾。

speaker: Unknown
en: What a run we're on.
zh: 我们这是多棒的历程啊。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've learned so much from Noah.
zh: 我从 Noah 那里学到了很多。

speaker: Unknown
en: We sat down to write product principles for Slack.
zh: 我们坐下来为 Slack 撰写产品原则。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Ethan Eisman, our head of design, was leading that initiative, but really this was a lot about codifying the early ideas that made Slack great, especially from a product principles and sort of craft perspective.
zh: Ethan Eisman，我们的设计主管，正在领导这项倡议，但这实际上主要是将使 Slack 变得伟大的早期想法正式化，特别是从产品原则和工艺角度来看。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we started talking about this and Noah had been at Slack early on, and he pulls out a small notebook that he kept in his bag and he said, "I actually have a section of this notebook that I've been keeping things I learned from Stuart over the years."
zh: 我们开始谈论这个，Noah 很早就加入了 Slack，他从包里拿出一个小笔记本，他说，"实际上我笔记本里有一部分，多年来我一直记录我从 Stuart 那里学到的东西。"

speaker: Unknown
en: Now, he hadn't been keeping that notebook because he said to himself, "Oh, one day we're going to write product principles, and I'm going to want these quotes on hand."
zh: 现在，他保留那个笔记本并不是因为他对自己说，"噢，有一天我们要写产品原则，我会想要这些语录在手边。"

speaker: Unknown
en: Or, "I'm going to show off that I have this."
zh: 或者，"我要炫耀我有这个。"

speaker: Unknown
en: No, it was for genuine learning and growth of his own ideas and place as a product leader that he was taking those.
zh: 不，这是为了他自己想法的真实学习和成长，以及作为产品领导者的位置，他才做了这些记录。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what it allowed him to do was, reflect back to Stuart ideas and principles that he had, but also grow his own product sense intuition.
zh: 这让他能够做的是，向 Stuart 反馈他有的想法和原则，但也培养他自己的产品感直觉。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as an organization, it then allowed us to scale those principles, and I think was a really, really important part.
zh: 作为一个组织，它然后让我们能够扩展这些原则，我认为这是一个非常、非常重要的部分。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we worked with a lot of the sort of early Slack folks and the people that had really developed that culture, but it was folks who had really tried to grow and learn.
zh: 我们与许多早期的 Slack 人以及真正培养那种文化的人合作过，但那些是真正试图成长和学习的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They weren't trying to politic, they were trying to build great products.
zh: 他们不是想搞政治，他们是想建立伟大的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that's the approach we have to take.
zh: 这就是我们必须采取的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what if you're not working for Stuart, which most people... Stuart also former podcast guests.
zh: 那么，如果你不是为 Stuart 工作，大多数人都不是……Stuart 也是前播客嘉宾。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like just the alumni of Slack, holy moly, just like this is a...
zh: 我感觉就像 Slack 的校友一样，天哪，这简直就是……

speaker: Unknown
en: We had a really good run.
zh: 我们有一段非常棒的历程。

speaker: Unknown
en: The fact that, just the fact that I have so many of these people on the podcast, what a sign of an incredible team.
zh: 事实上，仅仅是我有这么多这样的人在播客上，这真是一个不可思议的团队的标志。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So yeah, so most people do not work for a Stewart that has incredible product sense, really cares about craft.
zh: 所以是的，所以大多数人不是为一个 Stewart 工作，他有令人难以置信的产品感，真正关心工艺。

speaker: Unknown
en: And most people are like, "He or she is so wrong about what we're doing, this is such a mistake.
zh: 大多数人是这样的，"他或她对我们正在做的事情完全错了，这是一个错误。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't listen, they don't know what they're talking about."
zh: 他们不听，他们不知道自己在说什么。"

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you deal with that?
zh: 你怎么处理那个？

speaker: Unknown
en: One assumption I have of how folks may misunderstand this skill is that they have to be a yes man.
zh: 我对人们可能如何误解这项技能的一个假设是，他们必须做一个唯唯诺诺的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have to take everything that the leader said and do exactly that.
zh: 他们必须接受领导说的一切并完全照做。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is absolutely wrong.
zh: 那绝对是错误的。

speaker: Unknown
en: You get paid to have an opinion, you get paid to be a domain expert.
zh: 你拿薪水是为了有观点，你拿薪水是为了成为领域专家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in a lot of ways, your executive is looking for you to be the deepest person in the room, especially if they're good at their job, right?
zh: 在很多方面，你的高管希望你是房间里知识最渊博的人，特别是如果他们擅长自己的工作，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: They're looking for you to be the expert.
zh: 他们希望你成为专家。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, bringing your expertise to bear is absolutely crucial.
zh: 所以，运用你的专业知识绝对至关重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: Elon Frank, who was my boss at Slack as well, is now CPO of Checker, he always had a customer anecdote in his back pocket.
zh: Elon Frank，也是我在 Slack 时的老板，现在是 Checker 的 CPO，他总是会在兜里揣一个客户轶事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I can hear him in my head saying, "I was just on site last week with so-and-so and they said blah, blah, blah."
zh: 我脑海中能听到他说，“上周我刚和某某在现场，他们说这这那那。”

speaker: Unknown
en: He always brought his expertise to bear, and many of the executives at Slack were not from a strong enterprise background, so he could bring that to the forefront.
zh: 他总是运用他的专业知识，而且 Slack 的许多高管都不是出身于很强的企业背景，所以他能把这一点带到最前面。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that has to be married again with that curiosity and empathy.
zh: 我认为这必须与那种好奇心和同理心再次结合在一起。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you hear something that you don't agree with, this is something that a guy my team does super, super well right now.
zh: 如果你听到你不认同的事情，这是我的团队里现在有一个人做得非常、非常好的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: He'll hear something that flies in the face of the data he's seeing, the insights he's gotten, his experience in his domain expertise, and he'll say, "That's so interesting.
zh: 他会听到一些与他看到的数据、他获得的见解、他的领域专业经验相悖的事情，然后他会说，“这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What led you to believe that?"
zh: 是什么让你相信那一点？”

speaker: Unknown
en: That kind of question is actually curious, about this person said something that I think is dumb, but there must be something behind it.
zh: 这种问题实际上是好奇的，关于这个人说了一些我认为很愚蠢的话，但背后一定有什么东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so if I actually care what's behind it, and what you end up doing in that question is co-creating with the person who offered the opinion in the first place.
zh: 所以如果我真的在乎那背后是什么，而你在那个问题中最终做的是与最初提出那个观点的人共同创造。

speaker: Unknown
en: You're able to say to them, "I'm interested in what led you to that belief, your experience.
zh: 你能够对他们说，“我对是什么导致你产生那个信念、你的经历感兴趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: Did you have a meeting last week?
zh: 你上周有开会吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you getting pressure from the board right now in a certain way?
zh: 你现在是否正以某种方式受到董事会的压力？

speaker: Unknown
en: What is leading you to that belief?"
zh: 是什么让你产生那个信念？”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one thing that really trips people up is execs are so good at seeming certain.
zh: 我认为有一件事真的让人们绊跤，那就是高管们非常擅长看起来很确定。

speaker: Unknown
en: They say things in an authoritative way with confidence, with surety, because so much of their work is having to make those ultra-fast decisions with little information.
zh: 他们用权威的方式，带着自信、带着确定地说事情，因为他们工作的很大一部分是必须在信息很少的情况下做出那些超快的决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if this is someone who is also a learner, who also has a growth mindset, then if you are able to help them unpack why they believe something and then respond with your own domain expertise, you are able to get to a better solution together.
zh: 但如果这也是一个学习者，也有成长心态，那么如果你能够帮助他们拆解他们为什么相信某事，然后用你自己的领域专业知识回应，你就能够一起找到更好的解决方案。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love these very tactical phrases and tips for how to deal with someone, say a leader.
zh: 我喜欢这些非常战术性的短语和技巧，关于如何应对某人，比如说是领导者。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's just say this is like they're a director of product, VP of product, CEO that you're trying to convince to agree to plan.
zh: 我们就假设这就像他们是一个产品总监、产品副总裁、CEO，而你正试图说服他们同意一个计划。

speaker: Unknown
en: So the phrase you're suggesting here is, "That's so interesting.
zh: 所以你在这里建议的短语是，“这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What led you to believe that?"
zh: 是什么让你相信那一点？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Anything else along these lines, say you're pitching a product or an idea to say a VP of product and they're just like, "Hmm.
zh: 还有什么类似的说法，假设你正在向产品副总裁推销一个产品或一个想法，而他们只是像，“嗯。

speaker: Unknown
en: What else works?"
zh: 还有什么有效？”

speaker: Unknown
en: The earlier that you can understand the belief system and the things that are important to them, the better off you're going to be.
zh: 你越早能够理解他们的信念体系和对他们来说重要的事情，你的处境就会越好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So very, very early on in this process, what is top of mind for them?
zh: 所以在这个过程的非常、非常早期，什么在他们脑海中是最重要的？

speaker: Unknown
en: I can count on one hand when I was chief of staff, how many people asked me for advice before going into a product review with our CPO or our other executive team to say, "What do you think is most important to them right now?"
zh: 当我是幕僚长时，我可以扳着手指头数过来，有多少人在去和我们的 CPO 或其他高管团队进行产品评审之前向我征求建议，说，“你认为现在对他们来说最重要的是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: Use the people around them, their EA, their chief of staff, the people who have successfully pitched ideas in the past and say, "What worked?
zh: 利用他们周围的人，他们的 EA，他们的幕僚长，那些在过去成功推销过想法的人，并说，“什么奏效了？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you think they're worried about?
zh: 你认为他们在担心什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are the risks?"
zh: 风险是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And in an age of the tools of AI that we have, this is so much easier than it's ever been, right?
zh: 在我们拥有的 AI 工具的时代，这比以往任何时候都容易得多，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: You can ask Slackbot, "What has Rachel been posting about lately?
zh: 你可以问 Slackbot，“Rachel 最近在发什么帖子？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do you think is most important to her?"
zh: 你认为对她来说最重要的是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: A colleague of mine, a peer on our product leadership team, trained at GPT on a bunch of transcripts from past product reviews that are all publicly available, and we expect that our PMs are running their PRDs or their pitches through that, to say, "What's Rachel going to push back on?
zh: 我的一个同事，我们产品领导团队中的同僚，用一堆过去产品评审的记录稿训练了 GPT，这些记录稿都是公开可用的，我们期望我们的 PM 把他们的 PRD 或他们的推销方案通过那个模型，来问，“Rachel 会反驳什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where are their weaknesses in these ideas?"
zh: 这些想法的弱点在哪里？”

speaker: Unknown
en: You can also train things on, "Where are your own weaknesses?
zh: 你也可以根据这一点训练东西，“你自己的弱点在哪里？

speaker: Unknown
en: I know I've gotten feedback in the past that my data is a little thin or that this kind of UX thinking is thin.
zh: 我知道我在过去收到过反馈，说我的数据有点单薄，或者那种 UX 思维很单薄。

speaker: Unknown
en: Give me feedback on that."
zh: 给我关于那一点的反馈。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Claude's amazing for this.
zh: Claude 在这方面很神奇。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so, I think that the first step is actually just saying, what's important to them?
zh: 所以，我认为第一步实际上只是说，对他们来说什么很重要？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do they want to hear and anchor on that?
zh: 他们想听什么并以此为基础？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the second piece is going in to learn not to convince.
zh: 我认为第二部分是进去是为了学习而不是为了说服。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the earlier on you do that, the better.
zh: 而且你越早这样做越好。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we have something at Webflow called Office Hours.
zh: 所以我们在 Webflow 有一个叫 Office Hours 的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: As early as you can, you are having these conversations to align on strategic direction, maybe even before you have a one-pager.
zh: 尽可能早，你正在进行这些对话以对齐战略方向，甚至可能在你有一页纸之前。

speaker: Unknown
en: We implemented something at Slack with Stuart, because we realized we were coming to him with done designs and he was like, "What the fuck is this?
zh: 我们在 Slack 和 Stuart 实施了一些东西，因为我们意识到我们带着完成的设计去找他，而他会说，“这他妈是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: This is completely different than how I had this in my mind."
zh: 这和我脑子里想的完全不一样。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So Allie Rail, early Slack employee implemented something called, "Hey, Stuart, what do you think?"
zh: 所以 Allie Rail，Slack 的早期员工，实施了一种叫做，“嘿，Stuart，你怎么想？”的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we would just sit with him for half an hour and say, "What do you think on this topic?
zh: 我们会和他坐半个小时，说，“你在这个话题上怎么想？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your belief system?
zh: 你的信念体系是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's your past experience?"
zh: 你过去的经历是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And we started with a user interview, right?
zh: 我们是从用户访谈开始的，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: We started with, "We actually want to download your expertise here."
zh: 我们开始于，“我们实际上想在这里下载你的专业知识。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that when you engage execs in that way, it's really, really valuable.
zh: 我认为当你以那种方式与高管接触时，真的真的很有价值。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the things I see people do poorly is, they don't ask for the time.
zh: 我看到人们做得很差的一件事是，他们不争取时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is sort of in counterpoint to what we were talking about before with how busy their calendars are, but they're afraid to ask for more time to get that insight.
zh: 这有点反驳了我们之前关于他们的日程有多忙的讨论，但他们害怕争取更多时间来获得那个洞察。

speaker: Unknown
en: But sometimes if you don't, you miss the point.
zh: 但有时如果你不这样做，你就会错过重点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Something that we hear a lot on this podcast, is as a product leader, as a leader of any kind, you want to have a point of view, you want to come into a discussion with, "Here's our options, here's what I think is the right solution."
zh: 在这个播客中我们经常听到的一件事是，作为产品负责人，作为任何类型的领导者，你想要有一个观点，你想要带着“这是我们的选项，这是我认为正确的解决方案”进入讨论。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then at the same time, as you're describing, you want to come across as, I want to learn.
zh: 但与此同时，正如你所描述的，你想要表现出，我想学习。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm not here to convince you.
zh: 我不是来说服你的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm here to just help understand your worldview and where you think the right path is.
zh: 我只是来帮助理解你的世界观以及你认为正确的路径在哪里。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you think about just those two, that balance of, I have something I believe is right and, okay, but I'm here to learn and get your feedback and see what you think makes sense.
zh: 你如何看待这两者，即“我相信某事是对的”和“好的，但我是来学习并获得你的反馈并看看你认为什么是有意义的”之间的平衡。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So my advice on this may be a little paradoxical, because I think people make mistakes in both directions on the polls here.
zh: 所以我关于这个的建议可能有点自相矛盾，因为我认为人们在两边的极端上都犯了错误。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the one hand, I think there's an error people make of trying to show too much work, trying to tell too much upfront proof point.
zh: 一方面，我认为人们犯的一个错误是试图展示太多的工作，试图讲述太多预先设定的证明点。

speaker: Unknown
en: We talked to 16 participants from these 15 GOs and this is the statistically significant and the exec is like, "I'm so bored, I'm going to die."
zh: 我们与这 15 个 GO 中的 16 位参与者进行了交谈，这在统计上是显著的，而高管就像，“我好无聊，我要死了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I remember I did this in a review once and literally the person I was most trying to convince glazed over, got on their phone and I totally blew the meeting because I was trying to prove that I had done my homework.
zh: 我记得我在一次审查中这样做过，而我最想说服的那个人真的眼神呆滞，拿起了手机，而我完全搞砸了那次会议，因为我想证明我做了我的功课。

speaker: Unknown
en: Put it in the appendix, right?
zh: 把它放在附录里，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't need to know every single detail.
zh: 他们不需要知道每一个细节。

speaker: Unknown
en: The baseline expectation is that you did your job, and that this is built on a solid foundation of your domain expertise.
zh: 基本期望是你做了你的工作，并且这是建立在你的领域专业知识的坚实基础上的。

speaker: Unknown
en: On the flip side, I think there's some balance of showing your work, that helps elucidate why your solution is best.
zh: 另一方面，我认为在展示你的工作方面有一些平衡，这有助于阐明为什么你的解决方案是最好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So one of the mistakes I see people is doing is giving only one option.
zh: 所以我看到人们犯的一个错误是只给出一个选项。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that people, if you say, I mean, this is also like classic pricing and packaging strategy, give three options and the Goldilocks in the middle is the perfect one, right?
zh: 我认为人们，如果你说，我的意思是，这也像经典的定价和包装策略，给出三个选项，中间的那个 Goldilocks 就是完美的，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think that what that actually allows you to do is say, "Hey, we considered.
zh: 但我认为那实际上让你能够说的是，“嘿，我们考虑过了。

speaker: Unknown
en: We are not dumb.
zh: 我们不傻。

speaker: Unknown
en: We did not miss something.
zh: 我们没有遗漏什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: You think we missed something, but no, no, no, we actually considered this."
zh: 你认为我们遗漏了什么，但不，不，不，我们实际上考虑过这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So we had a recent product review with my manager, Rachel Woolen, she was a recent guest on How I AI, and-
zh: 所以我们最近与我的经理 Rachel Woolen 进行了一次产品审查，她是 How I AI 的最近一期嘉宾，并且——

speaker: Unknown
en: ... she was a recent guest on How I AI, and we brought a doc, a sort of reasonable approach to what we thought was the sort of problem and strategic space we were going after.
zh: ……她是 How I AI 的最近一期嘉宾，我们带了一份文档，一种对我们认为的问题类型和我们要追求的战略空间的合理方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: It didn't go well.
zh: 结果并不好。

speaker: Unknown
en: It really didn't match her expectations.
zh: 它真的不符合她的期望。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the feedback she gave us, which was really, really the right feedback, was I don't understand how you're thinking about the permutations if possible here because we had only given one option set.
zh: 她给我们的反馈，真的真的就是正确的反馈，是我不明白你如何考虑这里可能的排列组合，因为我们只给了一个选项集。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what we did following that review is we said, "Hey, we really want to sort this out.
zh: 所以我们在那次审查之后做的是，我们说，“嘿，我们真的想把这件事理清楚。

speaker: Unknown
en: We want to get moving.
zh: 我们想要开始行动。

speaker: Unknown
en: We want to show that we can really get building on this product.
zh: 我们想要表明我们真的可以在这个产品上开始构建。

speaker: Unknown
en: And alignment is the next stepping stone for that.
zh: 而对齐是下一步的基石。

speaker: Unknown
en: Can you meet in two days?"
zh: 你能在两天内见面吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And we turned around a new doc in two days that actually showed all of the options we had considered, but hadn't brought to that first review.
zh: 我们在两天内拿出了一份新文档，实际上展示了我们考虑过的所有选项，但在第一次审查中没有带来。

speaker: Unknown
en: And once we elucidated all the things we considered, and why we thought they would or wouldn't serve the outcomes we were driving and the technical complexity behind them, she was like, "Oh yeah, okay.
zh: 一旦我们阐明了我们考虑过的所有事情，以及为什么我们认为它们会或不会服务于我们推动的结果以及它们背后的技术复杂性，她就像，“哦是的，好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I see why the solution that you're proposing makes the most sense for what we're trying to accomplish."
zh: 我明白为什么你提出的解决方案对于我们要试图完成的事情最有意义了。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But in the first version, in the first meeting, we hadn't shown that and we hadn't workshopped it through her.
zh: 但在第一个版本中，在第一次会议中，我们没有展示这一点，也没有与她通过研讨会解决它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so sometimes really tactically you want to have that available to you.
zh: 所以有时非常战术性地你希望那个对你来说是可用的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not necessary to put all 15 options you considered, but at least be ready to show them.
zh: 放上你考虑过的所有 15 个选项并不是必要的，但至少准备好展示它们。

speaker: Unknown
en: Have them in an appendix, have them in a draft Figma file, whatever it is.
zh: 把它们放在附录里，把它们放在草稿 Figma 文件里，不管是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: We used to have this rule after a design review with Stuart.
zh: 我们过去在与 Stuart 的设计审查之后有这个规则。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'd go through the design review and he'd say, "No, do it exactly like this.
zh: 我们会进行设计审查，他会说，“不，完全按照这样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want the button here and the interaction to be this."
zh: 我想要按钮在这里，交互是这样的。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And frankly, he always had better ideas than we did, but we would come back and we called it Stuart plus two more.
zh: 坦率地说，他的主意总是比我们的好，但我们会回来，我们称之为 Stuart 加另外两个。

speaker: Unknown
en: So we did exactly what he asked for and then two other versions that we felt good about.
zh: 所以我们完全按照他的要求做了，然后是我们感觉很好的另外两个版本。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it gave us a forum for conversation to debate the merits of each type of approach.
zh: 然后它给了我们一个对话的论坛来辩论每种方法的优点。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's a bunch of advice here.
zh: 所以这里有一堆建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is the default is don't go in with, here's our whole process that landed on this suggestion, but have that ready because they may ask for that if they don't buy into it.
zh: 其中一个是，默认的做法是不要带着「这是我们得出这个建议的整个流程」进去，但要把这个准备好，因为他们如果不买账，可能会要求看这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Another is present options.
zh: 另一个是提供选项。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's, here's my point of view, here's why I think this is the right one.
zh: 然后还有，这是我的观点，这是我为什么认为这是正确做法的原因。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then if they dig in, you have to be ready for more options.
zh: 然后如果他们深究，你必须准备好更多的选项。

speaker: Unknown
en: Have you ever looked into the Minto Pyramid way of presenting stuff?
zh: 你有没有研究过 Minto Pyramid 的呈现方式？

speaker: Unknown
en: No, I don't know what that is.
zh: 不，我不知道那是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's this lady Barbara Minto.
zh: 所以有一位女士叫 Barbara Minto。

speaker: Unknown
en: She's like from the 50s, I think.
zh: 她好像是 50 年代的人，我想。

speaker: Unknown
en: She was the first female consultant at McKinsey or something like that.
zh: 她是麦肯锡的第一位女性顾问之类的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And she figured out the best way to present information to execs is backwards.
zh: 她发现了向高管展示信息的最佳方式是反向的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Essentially, it's instead of here's our process, here's all the things we did, here's our set of conclusions, and then here's our recommendation.
zh: 本质上，它不是「这是我们的流程，这是我们做的所有事情，这是我们的结论集合，然后这是我们的建议」。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's flip it, start with, here's our recommendation, and then here's the things we explored, and then here's the evidence behind that.
zh: 而是把它翻转，以「这是我们的建议」开始，然后「这是我们探索过的内容」，然后「这是其背后的证据」。

speaker: Unknown
en: It feels like that's what you're describing.
zh: 这感觉就像你在描述的那样。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's a really good way of doing it.
zh: 我认为这是一个非常好的做法。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think my only gloss on that would be that people are really different.
zh: 我认为我对此唯一的注解是，人是真的很不同的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Execs are people too.
zh: 高管也是人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so one of the things that I think is really important is understanding what is the literal format, storytelling, pre-work that is going to work for that person.
zh: 所以我认为非常重要的一件事是，理解什么是那种针对那个人的、真正有效的字面上的格式、讲故事、准备工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some people hate a PowerPoint and it's just going to crush their soul if you start doing that.
zh: 有些人讨厌 PowerPoint，如果你开始做那个，只会摧毁他们的灵魂。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some people only want to see the data and not the qualitative research.
zh: 有些人只想看到数据，而不是定性研究。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I do think it is about really understanding their communication style.
zh: 所以我确实认为这真的很需要了解他们的沟通风格。

speaker: Unknown
en: And execs, if you're listening to this, it's really fucking helpful if you tell people, because you actually know what you like and don't like.
zh: 还有高管们，如果你在听这个，如果你告诉人们，这真的非常有帮助，因为你实际上知道你喜欢什么和不喜欢什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so just tell people, "I prefer a doc.
zh: 所以就告诉人们，「我更倾向于文档。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want no upfront explanation.
zh: 我不要事前的讲解。

speaker: Unknown
en: I want 10 minutes of quiet reading time, and then we can come back together."
zh: 我要 10 分钟安静的阅读时间，然后我们可以回来一起讨论。」

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a particularly effective means for very busy people and short meetings, and especially in remote work where presentations just don't work very well.
zh: 这对于非常忙的人和短会议来说是一种特别有效的方式，特别是在远程工作中，演示文稿效果不是很好的情况下。

speaker: Unknown
en: Some execs are willing to do pre-read, some aren't.
zh: 有些高管愿意做预读，有些不愿意。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think you just have to be willing to accept that they are also human, and you're trying to give them the best shot at inculcating the information in the way that is meaningful to them.
zh: 我认为你只需要愿意接受他们也是人，你正在尝试给他们最好的机会，以对他们有意义的方式灌输这些信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's a bunch of tips here that I want to summarize for how to set yourself up for success.
zh: 所以这里有一堆技巧，我想总结一下如何为成功做好准备。

speaker: Unknown
en: One is what you just described, which is just think about how do they respond best?
zh: 其中一个是你刚刚描述的，也就是想一想他们反应最好的是什么方式？

speaker: Unknown
en: What kind of setup do they respond best to?
zh: 他们对什么样的设置反应最好？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it a presentation?
zh: 是演示文稿吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it a here's what we recommend, here's three options.
zh: 是「这是我们建议的，这里有三个选项」吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it data, something else?
zh: 是数据，还是别的东西？

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's just understand, here's what this person likes.
zh: 所以就是要了解，这就是这个人喜欢的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They want a five minutes fast conclusion.
zh: 他们想要五分钟的快速结论。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why?
zh: 为什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Then there's your advice of ask people that know them, what is top of mind for them right now?
zh: 然后还有你的建议，问那些认识他们的人，他们现在最关心的是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because they may be like, "This is the big bet we're making right now.
zh: 因为他们可能会说，「这是我们现在正在下的大赌注。

speaker: Unknown
en: If it's not aligned with that, they're not going to care."
zh: 如果不与那一致，他们就不会在乎。」

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's like either ask them or ask people around them, what's top of mind for them right now.
zh: 所以就像问他们或问他们周围的人，他们现在最关心的是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's this idea of simulate almost, simulate them, feed all the previous meetings with them into say a Claude project or something like that, create a little GPT and run your idea by them.
zh: 然后还有这个模拟的想法，几乎就是模拟他们，把与他们的所有之前的会议投喂到像 Claude 项目这样的东西里，创建一个小 GPT 并把你的想法拿给他们跑一遍。

speaker: Unknown
en: And like, what would you say?
zh: 然后像，你会说什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I will tell you, I am not a big fan of the what's top of mind for you question.
zh: 我要告诉你，我不是「你最关心的是什么」这个问题的粉丝。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is a hot take because I think that what often people do is they think through their top priorities, their top experiences.
zh: 这是一个激进的观点，因为我认为通常人们做的是思考他们的首要任务、他们的首要体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: If we were trying to do a research study to understand the day-to-day lives of our users, we probably wouldn't say what's top of mind for you.
zh: 如果我们要试图做一项研究来了解我们用户的日常生活，我们可能不会说「你最关心的是什么」。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'd say, "Tell me how you spent your day."
zh: 我们会说，「告诉我你是怎么度过你的一天的。」

speaker: Unknown
en: We'd say, "What's the most urgent priority for you right now that you're really scared about messing up?
zh: 我们会说，“你现在最紧迫的优先事项是什么，是你真的很害怕搞砸的？

speaker: Unknown
en: What pressures are you facing?"
zh: 你面临着什么压力？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think there's ways for us to ask much more interesting questions of our executives.
zh: 所以我认为，我们要向高管提出更有趣的问题，是有方法的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I recently did this with our CEO.
zh: 我最近和我们的 CEO 一起做了这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: I said, "Tell me what the board is pushing you on because everyone's got a boss.
zh: 我说，“告诉我董事会正在给你施压什么，因为每个人都有老板。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even a CEO who seems so powerful and competent and sure is getting pressures, right?
zh: 即使是一个看起来如此强大、胜任和确信的 CEO 也面临着压力，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are you seeing as the key inputs to your success?"
zh: 你认为你成功的关键输入是什么？”

speaker: Unknown
en: The other thing people don't realize is execs want to be successful too.
zh: 人们没有意识到的另一件事是，高管们也想要成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: They want to be good at their jobs.
zh: 他们想把自己的工作做好。

speaker: Unknown
en: And how can you help them?
zh: 你怎么能帮助他们？

speaker: Unknown
en: In the best case scenario, your incentives at a local team level or product organization level really closely align with their incentives that you're working on something that really matters to the company.
zh: 在最理想的情况下，你在本地团队层面或产品组织层面的激励措施，与他们对你正在做的事情真的很重要的激励措施非常一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're not, you really should be having that conversation because you'll be in misalignment from the start.
zh: 如果不一致，你真的应该进行那次谈话，因为从一开始你就不一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: But if you actually have alignment, point out that alignment.
zh: 但如果你真的有一致性，就要指出这种一致性。

speaker: Unknown
en: How is the metric that they're trying to move, the OKR they're responsible for, the board pressure that they're under going to be improved by the thing that you are proposing and how do you get there together?
zh: 他们试图推动的指标、他们负责的 OKR、他们面临的董事会压力，将如何通过你提出的事情得到改善，以及你们如何共同实现这一点？

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that one of the things people really miss out on here is what are the success criteria for an exec?
zh: 所以我认为，人们在这里真正错过的事情之一是，高管成功的标准是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And how do you model your world to amplify that success so that both of you can be more successful?
zh: 你如何构建你的世界模型来放大这种成功，以便你们两个都能更成功？

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that is such an important lesson before I follow that thread.
zh: 这是一个非常重要的教训，在我跟进这个话题之前。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just to clarify this question of what is top of mind, your advice was ask people around them, what is top of mind for them?
zh: 只是为了澄清这个关于“什么是首要关注点”的问题，你的建议是询问他们周围的人，什么是他们的首要关注点？

speaker: Unknown
en: Don't ask them directly this question.
zh: 不要直接问他们这个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Top of mind has become this sort of trope, right?
zh: 首要关注点已经变成了一种老生常谈，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's execs write a weekly writeup of what's top of mind.
zh: 高管们会写一份每周关于什么是首要关注点的总结。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it becomes this generic, uninteresting, neutralized frame.
zh: 它变成了一种通用的、无趣的、被中和的框架。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think the question, what's top of mind for you has actually lost some of its firepower.
zh: 所以我认为，“什么是你的首要关注点”这个问题实际上已经失去了一些火力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And instead, we have to get at what is the emotion, the drive behind it, and what's the spicier question that'll get a more interesting answer.
zh: 相反，我们必须触及其中的情感、背后的驱动力，以及那个能获得更有趣答案的更辛辣的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: So then going back to this incentives piece, such a powerful lever for basically to get anyone to agree to what you want, which is what are their goals?
zh: 那么回到这个激励措施部分，这是一个非常强大的杠杆，基本上可以让任何人同意你想要的，也就是他们的目标是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are they trying to do?
zh: 他们想做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: How are they measured?
zh: 他们是如何被衡量的？

speaker: Unknown
en: How is the success for them?
zh: 对他们来说成功是怎样的？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then how's the thing you're pitching them aligning with that and will help them achieve that specific goal?
zh: 然后你向他们推销的东西如何与之一致，并将帮助他们实现那个特定目标？

speaker: Unknown
en: And in building products together, your incentives should be making your users successful, making your business successful because of that.
zh: 在共同构建产品时，你的激励应该是让你的用户成功，并因此让你的业务成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so what do they actually believe about that?
zh: 那么他们对此真正相信什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not just about, oh, they want a promotion or they don't want to get fired or they want to hit their KR.
zh: 不仅仅是关于，哦，他们想要升职或者他们不想被解雇或者他们想要达成他们的 KR。

speaker: Unknown
en: Those are very, very real human things.
zh: 这些都是非常、非常真实的人性因素。

speaker: Unknown
en: But in an ideal world, in product work, it is really based on building incredible products for your users and therefore great outcomes for your business.
zh: 但在理想世界里，在产品工作中，它实际上是建立在为你的用户构建令人难以置信的产品，从而为你的业务带来巨大结果的基础上的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think a big part of this is actually understanding their strategic insights and what they believe is going to move the business forward most effectively.
zh: 所以我认为，这很大一部分实际上是理解他们的战略见解，以及他们相信什么能最有效地推动业务发展。

speaker: Unknown
en: So after I was chief of staff, I took over Slack's core product team.
zh: 所以在我担任幕僚长之后，我接管了 Slack 的核心产品团队。

speaker: Unknown
en: We worked on channels and emoji and search and messaging.
zh: 我们致力于频道、表情符号、搜索和消息传递。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there was a real feeling from our executive team that we had a bit lost our mojo around product craft.
zh: 我们的高管团队确实感觉到我们在产品工艺方面有点失去了魔力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we talked about painting the insides of the cabinets.
zh: 我们谈论了给柜子内部刷漆。

speaker: Unknown
en: We really wanted such an amazing experience for our users.
zh: 我们真的想为我们的用户提供如此惊人的体验。

speaker: Unknown
en: That was one of the things that made Slack great in the first place was just it really felt like a product that took that usability to a 10X place.
zh: 这起初是让 Slack 变得伟大的原因之一，就是它真的感觉像是一个把可用性提升了 10 倍的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there was a sense that I had heard from our leadership that we weren't quite there, and we had lost some of that.
zh: 我从我们的领导层那里感觉到，我们并不完全在那里，我们失去了一些那样的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that was so, so important to who we were and how we showed up for our users.
zh: 这对于我们是谁以及我们如何为用户服务来说是如此重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so we implemented something called the customer love sprint.
zh: 所以我们实施了一个叫做“客户爱冲刺”的项目。

speaker: Unknown
en: We said, "Okay, engineering, stop what you're doing.
zh: 我们说，“好的，工程师们，停下你们正在做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: As an entire team, we're going to spend two weeks just painting the inside of the cabinets."
zh: 作为一个完整的团队，我们要花两周时间只给柜子内部刷漆。”

speaker: Unknown
en: The only rule was engineers got to pick what they worked on.
zh: 唯一的规则是工程师可以选择他们工作的内容。

speaker: Unknown
en: We supported them from a PM and design and customer support perspective.
zh: 我们从 PM、设计和客户支持的角度支持他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: We gave them tons of ideas that engineers could pick what they worked on.
zh: 我们给了他们大量的想法，工程师可以选择他们做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: The only rule was that you had to ship something.
zh: 唯一的规则是你必须发布一些东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: You had to ship something that was good for users.
zh: 你必须发布一些对用户有益的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what we did is you could call this a bugbash.
zh: 我们所做的，你可以称之为 bugbash。

speaker: Unknown
en: You could say, "Okay, you got to fix these rough edges, these misses in the product."
zh: 你可以说，“好的，你必须修复这些粗糙的边缘，产品中这些缺失的地方。”

speaker: Unknown
en: But we made it a huge deal.
zh: 但我们把它搞得像件大事。

speaker: Unknown
en: We made it really exciting and fun, and we had a big, really fun judging competition that our executives took part of.
zh: 我们把它做得真的非常令人兴奋和有趣，我们有一个大型的、真正有趣的评审比赛，我们的高管也参与了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we really brought back that part of our culture that we had lost out on a bit.
zh: 我们真的找回了我们要一点失去的那部分文化。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it spoke to what our executives believed was a differentiation for us and how we were going to show up in the market, show up to our users, grow our user base by making it feel special.
zh: 这反映了我们的高管认为这是我们的一个差异化因素，以及我们如何在市场上展示，如何向我们的用户展示，通过让它感觉特别来增长我们的用户群。

speaker: Unknown
en: And we were able to say, "Oh, we shipped 65 improvements this week, this sprint."
zh: 我们能够说，“哦，这一周，这个冲刺，我们发布了 65 项改进。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And that felt really, really aligned with what they were trying to accomplish from a company perspective as well.
zh: 这感觉真的、真的与他们从公司角度试图实现的目标一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So to summarize the tactics so far, one is align your pitch with the person you're trying to pitch is incentives.
zh: 所以总结一下目前的策略，一是将你的推销与你要推销的对象的动机保持一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if the grammar on that sentence works, but what does success look like for them?
zh: 我不知道那个句子的语法是否通顺，但对来说成功是什么样子的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Connect the thing you're pitching them with that success.
zh: 将你向他们推销的东西与那个成功联系起来。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that aligning your incentives is, yes, really, really important in a product pitch.
zh: 我认为保持动机一致，是的，在产品推销中真的、真的非常重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're thinking about a product pitch, how does it connect to the company goals?
zh: 如果你在考虑产品推销，它是如何与公司目标联系起来的？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you trying to improve conversion rates?
zh: 你是在试图提高转化率吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you trying to grow enterprise customers?
zh: 你是在试图增长企业客户吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you trying to be perceived in brand?
zh: 你是在试图在品牌方面被认知吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Are you trying to break into some new category?
zh: 你是在试图进入某个新类别吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Of course, you have to align on a new pitch.
zh: 当然，你必须在一个新的推销上保持一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the inverse of this is how do you use that incentive structure to also inform regular roadmap, not just net new product ideas or net new pitches, but you're actually imbuing your entire team and your thought process with that incentive structure.
zh: 我认为反过来的是，你如何使用那个激励机制来也告知常规路线图，不仅仅是全新的产品想法或全新的推销，而且你实际上正在将你的整个团队和你的思维过程注入那个激励机制。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a deep understanding.
zh: 这是一个深刻的理解。

speaker: Unknown
en: How did the OKRs get to be the way they are?
zh: OKR 是怎么变成现在这样的？

speaker: Unknown
en: How did the positioning statement get to be that way?
zh: 定位声明是怎么变成那样的？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do I understand that and deeply embed it into my team's culture, so that we're reflecting what our exec team, our leadership has said is important?
zh: 我如何理解那个，并将其深深嵌入到我团队的文化中，以便我们反映出我们的高管团队、我们的领导层所说的什么是重要的？

speaker: Unknown
en: So basically everything should feel like it's a cohesive set of priorities based on some outcome y'all agreed to in the mission.
zh: 所以基本上，一切都应该感觉像是一套连贯的优先事项，基于你们在使命中商定的某些结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think a big part of this is also metrics and data and what you're actually measuring your own teams on.
zh: 我认为这很大一部分也是指标和数据，以及你实际测量你自己团队的是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: We talk a lot about leading and lagging indicators.
zh: 我们谈论很多关于先行指标和滞后指标。

speaker: Unknown
en: Or in the nonprofit world, this is called theory of change.
zh: 或者在非营利世界，这被称为变革理论。

speaker: Unknown
en: There are many steps.
zh: 有很多步骤。

speaker: Unknown
en: You yourself and your individual team may not be able to directly move enterprise revenue or directly move conversion rate, but you believe, and the company has a belief, that painting the inside of the cabinets, having an exceptional customer experience will be the thing that leads to customer retention, customer conversion, user satisfaction.
zh: 你自己和你个人的团队可能无法直接推动企业收入或直接推动转化率，但是你相信，并且公司有一个信念，即给橱柜内部刷漆、拥有卓越的客户体验将是导致客户留存、客户转化、用户满意度的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so how do you make sure that your metrics and the things that you are proposing to actually shift ladder up really, really clearly to those and that your executive agrees to that?
zh: 那么你如何确保你的指标和你提议实际转变的东西真的、真的清晰地层层递进到那些指标，并且你的高管也同意那一点？

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then a second lever is trying to not go into a conversation, even though you are trying to influence them.
zh: 然后第二个杠杆是试图不要带着目的进入对话，即使你正在试图影响他们。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's to go into it with an open mind and a learning mindset.
zh: 而是以开放的心态和学习心态进入它。

speaker: Unknown
en: You use this phrase.
zh: 你使用这个短语。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's so interesting.
zh: 那太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What led you to believe that?
zh: 是什么让你相信那一点？

speaker: Unknown
en: Right.
zh: 对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think in that is a disarming of the executive.
zh: 我认为这其中包含着对高管的解除武装。

speaker: Unknown
en: Again, going back to how do you get the best out of them?
zh: 再次回到，如何让他们发挥出最好的一面？

speaker: Unknown
en: You want them to feel comfortable.
zh: 你希望他们感到舒适。

speaker: Unknown
en: You want them to feel like they can be honest with you.
zh: 你希望他们觉得他们可以对你诚实。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think something I see people miss often is they don't follow the subtle threads that executives lead them, the sort of breadcrumbs of opinions.
zh: 我认为我看到人们经常错过的一点是，他们没有跟随高管引导他们的那些微妙的线索，那些观点的面包屑。

speaker: Unknown
en: In a more clear cut scenario, your leader, your executive will say to you, "Okay, I'd like a brief on exactly this thing.
zh: 在一个更明确的场景中，你的领导，你的执行官会对你说，“好的，我想要一份关于确切是这个事情的简报。

speaker: Unknown
en: Write it generally this way.
zh: 大体上按这种方式写。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's review it in a week."
zh: 我们在一周后审查它。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's a very clear cut ask.
zh: 这是一个非常明确的要求。

speaker: Unknown
en: But very, very often the asks are more subtle.
zh: 但很多时候，要求会更微妙。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wonder if I'm thinking about, have you considered?
zh: 我想知道我是否在想，你有没有考虑过？

speaker: Unknown
en: And what I find is that people don't take the bait.
zh: 我发现的是人们没有上钩。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the best people do.
zh: 最优秀的人会。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I'll give you an example.
zh: 所以我给你举个例子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just last week, we've been working a lot on as skills get democratized across Webflow, everyone can do design, everyone can ship code, everyone can write a PRD.
zh: 就在上周，我们一直在做很多工作，随着技能在 Webflow 上民主化，每个人都能做设计，每个人都能发布代码，每个人都能写 PRD。

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we enabling people to move as fast as their brains and tools will make them?
zh: 我们如何让人们能够像他们的大脑和工具所能达到的速度一样快速行动？

speaker: Unknown
en: Rachel, our CPO said, "Hey, Kev."
zh: 我们的 CPO Rachel 说，“嘿，Kev。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Our head of design, "We're going to have to think about design reviews at some point."
zh: 我们的设计主管说，“我们必须在某个时候考虑设计评审。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And within an hour, Kev had had a loom put together of, "Hey, here's a framework of high risk design changes, low risk design changes, blast radius, release processes, and how we might allow anyone in the organization to have designs shipped to production."
zh: 在一小时内，Kev 把一个 loom 放在一起，“嘿，这是一个高风险设计变更、低风险设计变更、爆炸半径、发布流程的框架，以及我们如何允许组织中的任何人将设计发布到生产环境。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That was a thread.
zh: 那是一个线索。

speaker: Unknown
en: She didn't ask him to do that.
zh: 她没有要求他那样做。

speaker: Unknown
en: He didn't need to follow up in that timeframe, but he recognized that the organization's incentives are to keep empowering people, to keep everyone moving fast, to keep everyone really excited about what they're building as well.
zh: 他不需要在那个时间范围内跟进，但他意识到组织的激励机制是继续为人们赋能，让每个人都保持快速行动，让每个人都对他们正在构建的东西真正感到兴奋。

speaker: Unknown
en: And he knew that he could slot in and respond to this feedback really, really quickly.
zh: 而且他知道他可以迅速介入并真正非常快地回应这个反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: A lot of times I see people don't take that invitation from the executive to actually engage on the subtle feedback they're giving.
zh: 很多时候我看到人们没有接受高管的这个邀请，去真正参与他们给出的微妙反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: An example from my past where this didn't go as well was we were in a strategy review, sort of a quarterly strategy review, and it was probably the fourth time I had heard Tamara ask, "I'm interested in seeing the top 10 use cases on X."
zh: 一个来自我过去的、进行得不那么顺利的例子是，我们在进行战略评审，某种季度战略评审，这可能是我第四次听到 Tamara 问，“我有兴趣看 X 的前 10 个用例。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And the fourth time it happened, she got really frustrated.
zh: 当第四次发生时，她真的非常沮丧。

speaker: Unknown
en: She said, "We've talked about this so many times before.
zh: 她说，“我们以前已经讨论过这么多次了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Why don't we have this documented?"
zh: 为什么我们还没有把这个记录下来？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's because people are not picking up on the cue that there's really something deep underneath.
zh: 我认为这是因为人们没有意识到这个线索，即底下真的有深层次的东西。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's really an important piece of feedback or a question that they're not pulling on.
zh: 确实有一个重要的反馈点或一个问题，他们没有抓住。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe you think that that's not an important thing to do, and then you need more information about why they think it's important.
zh: 也许你认为那不是一件重要的事情，然后你需要更多关于为什么他们认为这很重要的信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: Because if you just let it go and you don't respond to the feedback or respond to the ask, that person loses faith in you, that you're going to follow up on a thing that they believe is important.
zh: 因为如果你只是让它过去，你没有回应反馈或回应要求，那个人就会对你失去信心，认为你不会跟进一件他们认为重要的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it also sounds like a really good way to influence is you take the reins and here's where I think we could go with this, and here's the answer to your question.
zh: 而且这听起来也是一种非常好的影响方式，就是你接过缰绳，这是我认为我们可以做到的方向，这是你问题的答案。

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think if you're thinking about how do you be more senior, how do you show up in a way that is in a leadership mindset?
zh: 我认为如果你在思考如何变得更高阶，如何以领导思维模式出现？

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to act like a CPO.
zh: 你必须表现得像个 CPO。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to come in with that perspective.
zh: 你必须带着那个视角进来。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to come in with a solution.
zh: 你必须带着解决方案进来。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to follow the thread that they've asked about.
zh: 你必须跟进他们询问的线索。

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to do it quickly.
zh: 你必须快速地做。

speaker: Unknown
en: One of the missteps here is people treat these interactions as so high stakes.
zh: 这里的一个误区是人们把这些互动看得风险太高。

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes they are, but one of the ways that you fail at that interaction is you wait too long to engage on the feedback.
zh: 有时确实如此，但你在那种互动中失败的方式之一就是你等待太久才参与反馈。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you wait a week for the follow-up items that you discussed in the meeting, that exec has moved on.
zh: 如果你等了一周才去做你在会议上讨论的跟进事项，那位高管已经继续前进了。

speaker: Unknown
en: And you've missed your chance to actually respond to the feedback in a quick manner, you will get so much more excitement, enthusiasm, brain power from them if you keep the ball rolling in a timely manner.
zh: 而你已经错过了以快速方式真正回应反馈的机会，如果你及时地保持势头，你将从他们那里得到更多的兴奋、热情和脑力。

speaker: Unknown
en: And obviously, everyone's so busy.
zh: 显然，每个人都太忙了。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's already so much work on their plates.
zh: 他们盘子里已经有太多的工作了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You can't just hop on it.
zh: 你不能直接跳上去做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everything an exec says.
zh: 高管说的每一件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's also this classic advice of sometimes execs just say something and everyone takes it so seriously.
zh: 还有一种经典的建议，有时高管只是随口一说，而每个人都把它看得太严重了。

speaker: Unknown
en: "Oh, I have to do that."
zh: “噢，我得做那个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And sometimes they're just saying it in passing.
zh: 有时他们只是顺带一提。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's a balance of take the opportunities, don't [inaudible 00:42:13] single thing.
zh: 所以这是一种平衡，抓住机会，不要[听不清 00:42:13]每一件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And a great question to ask in that scenario is how strongly do you feel about this?
zh: 在这种情况下问的一个好问题是，你对这件事的感觉有多强烈？

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you hear something that they say really strongly, or how urgent do you think that is?
zh: 所以如果你听到他们说的某些话非常强烈，或者你认为这有多紧急？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or, "Hey, here's what the team is working on now.
zh: 或者，“嘿，这是团队现在正在做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you think that that trumps these priorities?"
zh: 你认为这会盖过这些优先事项吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And if they say," Yes, listen, do the thing that they say trumps it.
zh: 如果他们说，“是的，听着，做他们认为盖过它的事情。

speaker: Unknown
