speaker: Unknown
en: Just like say you have a big idea and they're like, "Nah," what are some expectations to set for people that come up with a big idea that just isn't getting any traction?
zh: 就像假设你有一个大想法，而他们说，“不”，对于提出一个大想法却没有任何牵引力的人，应该设定哪些期望？

speaker: Unknown
en: It really depends on what kind of organization you're working in.
zh: 这真的取决于你在什么样的组织中工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you're working in a smaller organization, if that big idea is something that you can build on your own or you can build within, versus if you need a large scale investment ... I think the real thing about building toward a big idea, towards something net new, the key is really outside and around the executive.
zh: 如果你在一个小型组织中工作，如果那个大想法是可以你自己构建的，或者你可以在内部构建的，对比如果你需要大规模投资……我认为致力于大想法、全新事物的关键，真正在于高管之外和高管周围。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if the executive is the decision maker on whether we're going to invest here, they also want to understand from their peers, from other experts, why this actually matters.
zh: 所以如果高管是我们是否在这里投资的决策者，他们也想从他们的同龄人、从其他专家那里了解为什么这真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you have something net new that you want to bring to market and customer success thinks it would really accelerate expansion rates, or the marketing team thinks that signups would be 10X because of this, if you have that cross-functional alignment, and this is where a lot of these lessons don't just apply if you're going into a product executive meeting, but that if you're influencing your cross-functional execs and your cross-functional stakeholders to be advocates on your behalf, I think that we now have more tools than ever to, as product folks, build out the V1, prototype something, build your own app, build the messy version of it, get feedback, iterate, and be socializing that in a way that creates a groundswell of buy-in to the point that the exec can't say no to the funding.
zh: 如果你有想要推向市场的全新东西，而客户成功团队认为这真的会加速扩张率，或者营销团队认为注册量会因此增加 10 倍，如果你有这种跨职能的一致性，这就是很多这些教训不仅适用于进入产品高管会议的地方，而且如果你你在影响你的跨职能高管和你的跨职能利益相关者成为你的倡导者，我认为我们现在拥有的工具比以往任何时候都多，作为产品人员，构建 V1，制作原型，构建你自己的应用程序，构建它的混乱版本，获得反馈，迭代，并以一种创造大量支持的方式社交化，以至于高管不能拒绝资助。

speaker: Unknown
en: Along those lines, it feels like to me, the biggest reason that a leader doesn't buy into your pitch is you just have different information.
zh: 沿着这些思路，对我来说，领导者不买账你的推介的最大原因就是你们拥有不同的信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't see what you see, you don't see what they see.
zh: 他们没看到你看到的，你没看到他们看到的。

speaker: Unknown
en: They have all this other stuff that they're looking at and trying to decide.
zh: 他们有所有其他这些东西在关注并试图决定。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe speak to just that skill of trying to clarify information on both sides.
zh: 也许谈谈试图澄清双方信息的技巧。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm thinking about a tool April Underwood taught me.
zh: 我在想 April Underwood 教我的一个工具。

speaker: Unknown
en: She said she used to go into product strategy meetings with Stewart and was really trying to extract some of his ideas, but she was always holding the whiteboard marker.
zh: 她说她过去常带着 Stewart 参加产品战略会议，真的试图提取他的一些想法，但她总是拿着白板记号笔。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so there was this back and forth between them in the conversation of, I'm trying to understand deeply what you're seeing in the market, what you're feeling in the business, what your instincts are, but I'm also going to package that in a way that is translatable, is actionable, is in a framework that you might not have had in your head.
zh: 所以他们在对话中有着这种来回，我试图深入理解你在市场上看到的，你在业务中感受到的，你的直觉是什么，但我也会以一种可翻译的、可操作的、你可能脑海中没有的框架来包装它。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so that is like a great tool for marrying that leader's instincts with your ability to sort of not synthesize their approach, but really take that approach and accelerate it for marrying what you know about the world and what they know about the world.
zh: 所以这是一个很好的工具，可以将领导者的直觉与你的能力结合起来，而不是综合他们的方法，而是真正采用这种方法并加速它，以结合你了解的世界和他们了解的世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: So what I'm hearing is take it upon yourself to help them to kind of align your world view with their world view.
zh: 所以我听到的是，你要主动去帮助他们，让你的世界观与他们的世界观保持一致。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's this question coming back just like, "Help me understand what I don't ... What was the question you had?
zh: 这个问题又回来了，比如，“帮我理解我不懂的……你有的问题是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, that's so interesting.
zh: 哦，这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What led you to believe that?"
zh: 是什么让你相信那一点？”

speaker: Unknown
en: These are the kind of questions that help you extract what you don't know that they know.
zh: 这些是那种帮助你提取出你不知道但你知道的事情的问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there are also really different phases of a relationship.
zh: 我认为关系中也有非常不同的阶段。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a phase when you're new, you may not have so much face time with that person, you don't really know them that well, but I think that this is a place where time spent together really just accelerates your trust with them, your ability to speak freely, to ask difficult questions, to get real feedback.
zh: 有一个阶段是你刚进来时，你可能没有那么多与那个人面对面的时间，你不太了解他们，但我认为这是一个你可以通过与他们共处的时间来真正加速你与他们的信任、你自由交谈的能力、提出困难问题和获得真实反馈的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so especially if you're in a position where you can ask for that time, more casual time, less fraught, that you're not always pitching an idea, but you can have real conversations, that is also the groundwork for the pitch down the line.
zh: 所以特别是如果你处于一个可以要求那个时间的职位，更随意的时间，更少担忧，你并不总是在推销一个想法，但你可以有真正的对话，这也是后来推销的基础。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think people devalue that, that time upfront spent together, because it pays dividends for the later pitch.
zh: 我认为人们低估了这一点，低估了前期花在一起的时间，因为它在以后的推销中会产生红利。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm glad you went there.
zh: 我很高兴你谈到了这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's exactly where I was going to actually go is this idea of trust and building and giving the person a reason to assume that you know what you're doing.
zh: 这实际上正是我要去的地方，就是这个信任和建立的想法，并给那个人一个理由去假设你知道自己在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Obviously hard to build trust.
zh: 显然建立信任很难。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the reason I think this is important is sometimes you have the best idea and they just don't buy it because they just don't know anything about ... They don't know what you've been up to.
zh: 我认为这很重要的原因是，有时你有最好的想法，但他们就是不买账，因为他们根本不了解……他们不知道你一直在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't know how awesome you are, how smart you are, how successful you've been your whole life and career, maybe because you're new.
zh: 他们不知道你有多棒，你有多聪明，你整个生活和职业生涯有多成功，可能是因为你是新来的。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are some tips for trying to build trust with the leaders so that these things become easier?
zh: 有什么建议可以尝试与领导者建立信任，以便这些事情变得更容易？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think in terms of building trust, it's really important to ground yourself in what they believe.
zh: 我认为在建立信任方面，让自己立足于他们所相信的事情真的很重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you are pitching an idea that is wildly different than something they believe, don't bother.
zh: 如果你推销的想法与他们相信的某事完全不同，那就别费心了。

speaker: Unknown
en: You probably have nine other good ideas that are more aligned with the beliefs that they already hold.
zh: 你可能有九个其他好的想法，更符合他们已经持有的信念。

speaker: Unknown
en: Follow those instead.
zh: 改而追求那些。

speaker: Unknown
en: So there's something about just engaging from a baseline assumption.
zh: 所以这只是关于从基准假设出发进行接触。

speaker: Unknown
en: If they feel strongly about something, okay, maybe you can come back to them once or twice and say, "Are you sure you feel strongly about that?
zh: 如果他们对某事感觉强烈，好吧，也许你可以回到他们那里一两次并说，“你确定你对那个感觉强烈吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I really see this data."
zh: 我真的看到了这个数据。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And if they say, "Yeah, I feel really strongly," you probably have to drop it.
zh: 如果他们说，“是的，我感觉非常强烈，”你可能不得不放弃它。

speaker: Unknown
en: But in terms of building that long-term trust, I think the biggest things that do that are really hearing them out and actioning on the feedback that you've gotten in the past.
zh: 但在建立那种长期信任方面，我认为能做到这一点的最大事情真的是倾听他们并根据你过去得到的反馈采取行动。

speaker: Unknown
en: Having results.
zh: 拥有结果。

speaker: Unknown
en: It cannot be overstated that impact in the organization, building great products, shipping amazing things quickly, feeding that back to the leader to say, "Hey, we worked with you on this.
zh: 怎么强调都不为过的是组织中的影响，构建伟大的产品，快速发布令人惊叹的东西，将其反馈给领导者说，“嘿，我们与你合作做了这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: We shipped it.
zh: 我们发布了它。

speaker: Unknown
en: It went well.
zh: 它进行得很顺利。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's the results."
zh: 这是结果。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That builds you momentum to be able to go back with the more novel approach or the scarier one.
zh: 那会为你建立动量，以便能够用更新的颖方法或更可怕的方法回去。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the other thing I would say is one of my favorite ... Well, actually one of the only business books I've ever liked is called Switch.
zh: 我想说的另一件事是，我最喜欢的之一……嗯，实际上我唯一喜欢过的商业书之一叫《Switch》。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's about change management, and they talk a lot about shrinking the change, which is this brilliant idea.
zh: 它是关于变革管理的，他们谈到了很多关于缩小变革，这是一个很棒的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: If something seems scary and overwhelming, how do you make it so much smaller so that it's an experiment?
zh: 如果某事看起来可怕且势不可挡，你怎么把它做得如此之小以至于它是一个实验？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's a one week proof of concept.
zh: 这是一个为期一周的概念验证。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's something that feels really small that can get people over the hump of, "Well, I'm not going to invest in six months for this project if I don't know if it's going to work."
zh: 这是一件感觉真的很小的事情，可以让人们克服障碍，“嗯，如果我不知道这个项目是否会成功，我就不会为此投资六个月。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So shrinking the change is a huge way to build momentum and trust.
zh: 所以缩小变革是建立动量和信任的巨大方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think this applies to the way that we develop all products, especially if you're doing something that lasts longer than a month.
zh: 我认为这适用于我们开发所有产品的方式，特别是如果你在做持续时间超过一个月的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are your milestones?
zh: 你的里程碑是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What are the chunks at which you're showing outcomes and fit?
zh: 你展示结果和适应性的块是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And this is easier than ever to do with the tools that we have at our disposal.
zh: 有了我们可以使用的工具，这比以往任何时候都容易做到。

speaker: Unknown
en: How are we showing that we are taking a much more iterative approach so that you can build that momentum, build that buy-in, and get that trust to do the bigger thing?
zh: 我们如何展示我们正在采取一种更迭代的方法，以便你可以建立那个动量，建立那个买进，并获得做更大事情的信任？

speaker: Unknown
en: That's an awesome, very tactical piece of advice here to convince someone to do something, is just reduce the risk essentially, reduce the investment to help build trust in, okay, this is showing success because obviously it's much easier to bite off on something that's going to take two weeks or-
zh: 这是一个非常棒的、非常战术性的建议，用来说服某人做某事，就是基本上降低风险，降低投资以帮助建立信任，好吧，这正在展示成功，因为显然如果是两周的事情或者-

speaker: Jessica Fain
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: ... have a very low risk and impact if it's not a good idea.
zh: ……如果这不是一个好主意，那么具有非常低的风险和影响。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah, I think that's absolutely right.
zh: 是的，我认为这完全正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think what is the type of risk that they are most afraid of too?
zh: 我想他们最害怕的风险类型是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it wasting time?
zh: 是浪费时间？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it time to market?
zh: 是上市时间？

speaker: Unknown
en: Is it that they don't actually think this is going to work with customers?
zh: 是不是他们实际上不认为这对客户行得通？

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you can start to, and you can and should ask this, what are the outcomes you're most afraid of?
zh: 所以如果你能开始，你可以也应该问这个问题，你最害怕的结果是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: What would a failure state be for you in this experiment?
zh: 在这个实验中，对你来说失败的状态会是什么样？

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a concept of red teaming, which is originally a military tactic and now being applied to businesses.
zh: 有一个红队测试的概念，它最初是一种军事战术，现在被应用到商业中。

speaker: Unknown
en: How do you take an outsider's perspective to your idea and not just be so obsessed with your own thinking, with your own concept?
zh: 你如何对你的想法采取局外人的视角，而不只是如此痴迷于你自己的想法，你自己的概念？

speaker: Unknown
en: Because so often we get wrapped around the axle, especially ... I actually find this happens even more with prototyping.
zh: 因为我们经常不知所措，尤其是……我实际上发现这在做原型时发生得更多。

speaker: Unknown
en: You build a prototype and you're like, "Oh, it looks so beautiful.
zh: 你构建了一个原型，你会觉得，“哦，它看起来太美了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I would've never been able to build that before."
zh: 我以前绝不可能构建出那个东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: It's actually not a very good idea, but you get obsessed with it and you're unwilling to accept that it could fail.
zh: 这实际上不是一个很好的主意，但你迷上了它，并且不愿意接受它可能会失败的事实。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one of the biggest things you can do to build trust is kill things, deprioritize things.
zh: 我认为建立信任你能做的最重要的事情之一就是砍掉东西，降低优先级。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is a very, very senior way of thinking, and it shows that you have the same aligned incentives as the executive who's thinking about the good of the company outcome, the user outcome, and not just your own.
zh: 这是一种非常、非常资深的思维方式，它表明你与那位正在考虑公司结果利益、用户结果利益，而不仅仅是你自己利益的高管有着一致的激励。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is such a good one because everyone's always just trying to acquire more resources, more investment, just like more features and showing that, "Okay, this is a terrible idea.
zh: 这是一个非常好的观点，因为大家总是试图获取更多资源、更多投资，就像更多功能一样，并表明，“好吧，这是一个糟糕的主意。

speaker: Unknown
en: I know I spent months on this, but no, we should kill this because it's not working."
zh: 我知道我在这个上花了几个月，但不，我们应该砍掉这个，因为它不起作用。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Right.
zh: 对。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's not working.
zh: 它不起作用。

speaker: Unknown
en: And here's how I'm going to know that it's not working.
zh: 这是我要知道它不起作用的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's when I'm going to come back to you with a decision.
zh: 这是我将带着决定回来的时间。

speaker: Unknown
en: So sometimes we don't know that.
zh: 所以有时我们不知道那个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Well, a lot of times we don't know the outcome, especially with AI native products.
zh: 嗯，很多时候我们不知道结果，尤其是对于 AI 原生产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if we can say, "We're going to test this.
zh: 如果我们能说，“我们要测试这个。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's how we're going to know it's working.
zh: 这是我们知道它在起作用的方法。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'll come back to you on X date."
zh: 我们会在 X 日期给你回音。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think one thing that really spirals people out of control is a desire for certainty.
zh: 我认为真正让人失控的一件事是对确定性的渴望。

speaker: Unknown
en: As human beings, many of us have a huge desire for certainty.
zh: 作为人类，我们中的许多人都有巨大的对确定性的渴望。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now more than ever, that is not possible.
zh: 现在比以往任何时候，这都是不可能的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so one trick that I learned, especially working with engineers who often have a very, very high desire for certainty, is to say, "I don't know the answer right now.
zh: 所以我学到一个技巧，尤其是与那些通常有非常高确定性渴望的工程师一起工作时，是说，“我现在不知道答案。

speaker: Unknown
en: I don't know if this is going to be successful.
zh: 我不知道这是否会成功。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm going to check in with you on X date.
zh: 我会在 X 日期跟你检查。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's when we're going to come back and have another conversation."
zh: 那时我们会回来进行另一次谈话。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And so at least there's certainty about the next check-in point, and that can be a really powerful tool for deescalating the fears and risks around a given initiative or idea.
zh: 所以至少对下一次检查点有确定性，这可能是一个非常强大的工具，用于缓和围绕给定举措或想法的恐惧和风险。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get into AI's impact and all this stuff, are there any other common mistakes people make when they are trying to get better at the skill of influence or just any other really powerful tactics for increasing your ability to influence leaders?
zh: 在我们深入 AI 的影响和所有这些事情之前，当他们试图提高影响力技能时，人们还会犯其他常见的错误吗，或者还有什么真正强大的战术来提高你影响领导者的能力吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Something people miss is the extremely broad context that execs bring to bear from not only their experiences, but the things that they are hearing in their day-to-day life.
zh: 人们错过的一件事是高管们带来的非常广泛的背景，不仅来自他们的经验，还有他们在日常生活中听到的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: They understand what other product teams are working on better than you.
zh: 他们比你更了解其他产品团队在做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: They understand the conversation at the e-staff level.
zh: 他们了解 e-staff 层面的对话。

speaker: Unknown
en: They've been to executive round tables and [inaudible 01:03:08].
zh: 他们参加过高管圆桌会议和[听不清 01:03:08]。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of your jobs is to extract that insight and information and apply it to your ideas.
zh: 你的工作之一是提取这些洞察和信息并将其应用到你的想法中。

speaker: Unknown
en: And they want to feel like you care, like you respect that, like you can apply it to your own thinking.
zh: 他们想要感觉到你在乎，你尊重那个，你可以将其应用到你的思考中。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so this keeps coming back to that curiosity and empathy.
zh: 所以这总是回归到那种好奇心和同理心。

speaker: Unknown
en: What do they know that you don't, that you can actually apply to strengthen your thinking or inform the direction that you want to take your team?
zh: 他们知道什么你不知道的，你可以实际应用它来加强你的思考或告知你想带领团队的方向？

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think people just often don't ask those things of their executives.
zh: 我认为人们通常不向他们的高管问这些事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: They don't show a curiosity for the expertise that that person has brought to bear.
zh: 他们不对那个人带来的专业知识表现出好奇心。

speaker: Unknown
en: And frankly, I think often they don't really care, which is the chief mistake.
zh: 坦率地说，我认为通常他们并不在乎，这是主要的错误。

speaker: Unknown
en: They should really actually take it into account to strengthen their own thinking.
zh: 他们实际上应该将其考虑在内以加强他们自己的思考。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So much of this is about just coming into it with like, here's what I ... It's just like having the same information.
zh: 很大一部分只是带着这样的心态进入，这就是我……这就像拥有相同的信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there's a point about just believe they know what they're doing is so underappreciated.
zh: 我认为有一点只是相信他们知道自己在做什么被严重低估了。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a reason they're in this role.
zh: 他们在这个角色是有原因的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a reason you're not there.
zh: 你不在那个位置是有原因的。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a reason this company's doing as well as it is.
zh: 这家公司做得这么好是有原因的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even if they are wrong, they believe they're right.
zh: 即使他们错了，他们也认为自己是对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So it's important to understand what they think and know and what information they have access to.
zh: 所以重要的是理解他们想什么、知道什么，以及他们能获取什么信息。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the other piece of this is for anyone thinking about how they grow their career, how did that person get into the role that they are in?
zh: 我认为另一部分是，对于任何考虑如何发展职业生涯的人来说，那个人是如何进入他们目前所在角色的？

speaker: Unknown
en: They broadened their context.
zh: 他们拓宽了他们的背景。

speaker: Unknown
en: They saw, sort of had great peripheral vision, and that is a way to show up in a room that is much more senior than you are, is keep your domain expertise, keep the things that you know best, but broaden your perspective, broaden your vantage point to think about it in the context of the whole ecosystem, the whole organization, the whole industry, which you often don't get a chance to stay in.
zh: 他们拥有，可以说是很好的周边视野，这是一种在一个比你资历高得多的房间里表现自己的方式，就是保持你的领域专长，保持你最擅长的东西，但拓宽你的视角，拓宽你的有利位置，在整个生态系统、整个组织、整个行业的背景下思考问题，这通常是你没有机会去做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what happens at that point is you get seen as a strategic thinker.
zh: 在那个时候发生的事情是，你被视为一位战略思考者。

speaker: Unknown
en: You get seen as somebody who really cares about the company's success and not just your own.
zh: 你被视为一个真正关心公司成功而不仅仅是自己成功的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it is actually a really, really important tool for growth, especially ... I had told April when I originally pitched her on being our chief of staff that I wanted to take the job because I wanted to know if I wanted to be a CPO when I grew up.
zh: 因此，这实际上真的是一个非常重要的成长工具，尤其是……我最初告诉 April 我想做我们的幕僚长时，我说我想接这份工作是因为我想知道我长大后是否想成为一名 CPO。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's true.
zh: 这是真的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I still don't know because it's a really, really hard job and I have just way more respect than I ever did before that.
zh: 我仍然不知道，因为这是一份真的很难的工作，而且我比以前任何时候都更尊重它。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I think that if you want to grow into a product leadership role, expanding your viewpoint, expanding the way that you see problems, and thinking about it from a global perspective is a way to show up as that strategic leader that you say you want to be.
zh: 但我认为，如果你想成长为产品领导者角色，拓宽你的观点，拓宽你看待问题的方式，并从全球角度思考，这是一种成为你声称自己想成为的那种战略领导者的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love this advice.
zh: 我喜欢这个建议。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just to make it very clear for people, because it's easy to just like, "Yeah, okay, I'll just expand my horizon," basically, the advice here is just think on behalf of the entire business, not just your feature, not just your little goal.
zh: 为了让人们非常清楚，因为这很容易就像这样，“好的，好吧，我就拓展一下我的视野”，基本上，这里的建议就是代表整个业务去思考，而不仅仅是你的功能，不仅仅是你那个小目标。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's like, what is the business trying to do?
zh: 就像是，业务想要做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's how this fits into it.
zh: 这就是它是如何融入其中的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's what the CEO broadly is thinking about.
zh: 这就是 CEO 广泛在思考的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: The epitome of this to me is Jeff Weinstein, I think is how you pronounce his last name, or Weinstein, at Stripe.
zh: 对我来说，这方面的典型是 Jeff Weinstein，我觉得他的姓氏是这么发音的，或者 Weinstein，在 Stripe。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's just like a PM on a product and he's just constantly tweeting and sharing Stripe as a business.
zh: 他只是产品上的一个 PM，他只是不断地发推文和分享 Stripe 作为一项业务。

speaker: Unknown
en: He's just thinking about Stripe as the company and then here's this thing I work on that helps the company.
zh: 他只是把 Stripe 作为一家公司来思考，然后这是我正在做的一件事，它可以帮助公司。

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And Jeff and I worked together at Box.
zh: Jeff 和我曾在 Box 共事。

speaker: Lenny
en: Oh, you did? Oh, that is so cool.
zh: 噢，你们有过？噢，那太酷了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: So would you agree he's like the epitome of this?
zh: 所以你同意他就是这方面的典型吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh yeah.
zh: 噢是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mean, he's so charismatic and able to really put himself in other people's shoes.
zh: 我的意思是，他非常有魅力，并且真的能够设身处地为他人着想。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that it is just a way to ... We just redid our PM career ladder for this year and I led that effort.
zh: 我认为这只是一种方式……我们刚刚重新制定了今年的 PM 职业阶梯，我主导了这项工作。

speaker: Unknown
en: And one of the things that we noted as a trait of becoming more senior is your product citizenship and how much you are embedding back in the organization and giving back best practices, mentoring others, broadening people's horizons on tooling, technology, storytelling, and of course strategy.
zh: 我们注意到的一个成为更资深人士的特征是你的产品公民意识，以及你在多大程度上回馈组织，回馈最佳实践、指导他人、拓宽人们在工具、技术、讲故事方面的视野，当然还有战略。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that this is such an important part of people's growth that they often undersell, but you can do it at every level in your own area.
zh: 我认为这是人们成长中如此重要的一部分，但他们经常低估，但你可以在你自己的领域的每个级别都做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's about connecting the dots back up to what is important to the company.
zh: 这是关于把这些点连接回对公司重要的事情上。

speaker: Unknown
en: So many times I see docs that are like, "Okay, our KR is to ship this thing."
zh: 很多时候我看到这样的文档，“好的，我们的 KR 是发布这个东西。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I'm like, "Why does that matter?
zh: 然后我就想，“为什么这很重要？

speaker: Unknown
en: Does anyone care?"
zh: 有人在在乎吗？”

speaker: Unknown
en: You have to tell a story.
zh: 你必须讲一个故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: And even if that's true, that shipping is the care, sometimes that's the case, right?
zh: 即使那是真的，发布就是那个关键，有时就是这种情况，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: But even if that's true, why are you doing that?
zh: 但即使那是真的，你为什么要那样做？

speaker: Unknown
en: What is the outcome for the business?
zh: 对业务来说结果是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: Why would the CEO be excited about shipping that?
zh: 为什么 CEO 会对接手发布那个东西感到兴奋？

speaker: Unknown
en: It's usually because of some business outcome or user outcome that you have to tie back to their perspective of.
zh: 通常是因为某种业务结果或用户结果，你必须把它联系回他们的视角。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then I think the flip side of is if you're working on something that doesn't shift one of those outcomes or isn't urgent for the business, do you need to change course?
zh: 然后我认为另一方面是，如果你正在做某种不会改变这些结果之一的事情，或者对业务来说并不紧迫，你需要改变方向吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Do you need to say, "Hey, actually, I don't think this is the highest leverage thing right now.
zh: 你需要说，“嘿，实际上，我认为这不是现在最高杠杆的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: We could actually do X to be much more effective for our broader goals."
zh: 我们实际上可以做 X 来为我们的更广泛目标更有效。”

speaker: Unknown
en: That's where I was going to go, is just like that's the ultimate trust building move is to tell the leaders, "Okay, here's our goal, here's all these ideas, but we don't think this is the right goal and here's how this doesn't fit in and here's why we should kill this product."
zh: 这正是我要说的，这就是建立信任的终极举措，就是告诉领导者，“好的，这是我们的目标，这是所有这些想法，但我们不认为这是正确的目标，这是它如何不匹配，这就是我们应该杀掉这个产品的原因。”

speaker: Unknown
en: Right, exactly.
zh: 对，完全正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: Right.
zh: 对的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Think like a CPO.
zh: 像个 CPO 一样思考。

speaker: Unknown
en: Put yourself in their shoes.
zh: 设身处地为他们着想。

speaker: Unknown
en: You had Stewart on a few weeks ago and he talked about how the CEO is kind of the only one who can really kill things, things that already exist, or really make some of those super, super tough decisions.
zh: 你几周前请来了 Stewart，他谈到 CEO 有点像是唯一一个能真正终结事物，即已经存在的事物，或者真正做出一些超级、超级艰难的决定的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that is oftentimes our culture, but is a mistake.
zh: 我认为这往往是我们的一种文化，但这是一种错误。

speaker: Unknown
en: If we each think of ourselves as owners and as contributors to the business and as leaders, we will bring those opportunities up as well to both amplify the business or save it from itself.
zh: 如果我们每个人都把自己视为老板，视为业务的贡献者，视为领导者，我们也会提出这些机会，来以此扩大业务或挽救业务本身。

speaker: Lenny
en: Do the job you want versus the job you have. Classic.
zh: 做你想做的工作，而不是你现有的工作。经典的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yes.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Let's talk AI.
zh: 让我们来谈谈 AI。

speaker: Unknown
en: I feel like you cannot do anything these days without mentioning AI in some way.
zh: 我觉得现在如果不以某种方式提到 AI，你就什么事也做不了。

speaker: Unknown
en: How is AI changing the skill of influence?
zh: AI 是如何改变影响力这项技能的？

speaker: Unknown
en: What's changed in the past couple years in trying to convince people to do the things you're hoping them to do?
zh: 在过去几年里，在试图说服人们做你希望他们做的事情方面，发生了什么变化？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that we are entering a golden age of product management, not of product managers, but of product management and the core skills that made this function the thing that I love to do.
zh: 我认为我们正在进入产品管理的黄金时代，不是产品经理的黄金时代，而是产品管理的黄金时代，也是核心技能的黄金时代，这些核心技能让这项职能成为了我喜欢做的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: More than ever, what we have to do is have really interesting ideas that are grounded in user empathy, curiosity, testing, iteration, but we will still be working in organizations where getting buy-in to those ideas and influencing people to fund not just the V1, but the much more expensive V2, three, four, and ongoing support become 10x more important.
zh: 比以往任何时候都更重要的是，我们要做的是提出真正有趣的想法，这些想法要植根于用户同理心、好奇心、测试和迭代，但我们仍然要在组织内工作，在这些组织中，让这些想法获得认可，并影响人们为不仅是 V1 版本，而且是为更昂贵的 V2、V3、V4 版本以及持续支持提供资金，这变得重要了 10 倍。

speaker: Unknown
en: And as execution basically plummets in complexity and everyone can be a builder, PMs and product thinkers, which I think is far beyond the product management function, can be so incredibly influenced by those core skills of bringing people along for the ride.
zh: 而且随着执行在复杂性上急剧下降，每个人都可以成为构建者，产品经理和产品思考者——我认为这远远超出了产品管理的职能——可以受到这些核心技能的不可思议的影响，即带领人们踏上这段旅程。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think that product managers for a long time have made their careers on being the most type A, the Gantt chart master, the best note taker in the business.
zh: 所以我认为产品经理长期以来通过成为 A 型性格最强烈的人、甘特图大师、业务中最好的会议记录员来成就他们的职业生涯。

speaker: Unknown
en: And if AI is better than you at analyzing data or taking meeting notes or running experiments, what's your job now?
zh: 如果 AI 在分析数据、做会议记录或运行实验方面比你更强，你现在的工作是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: So the leverage actually shifts from doing that work and being the synthesizer to deciding what work actually survives and encouraging other people to buy into that process.
zh: 所以杠杆实际上从做那项工作和成为综合者转移到了决定哪项工作真正存活下来，并鼓励其他人接受那个过程。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I actually think it's just so incredibly exciting because PMs and other functions, engineers, designers are so empowered to bring that first version to bear to get that momentum, to get that signal on product market fit, on user benefit, and then build momentum from that point in a much faster cycle.
zh: 我实际上认为这确实是非常令人兴奋的，因为产品经理和其他职能、工程师、设计师都非常有能力带出第一个版本，获得那种动力，获得关于产品市场契合度、用户价值的信号，然后从那时起建立动力，以一个更快的周期进行。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so it's actually an incredibly exciting place, but the act of influence, the act of stakeholder management, the act of learning...
zh: 所以这实际上是一个非常令人兴奋的地方，但是影响力的行为、利益相关者管理的行為、学习的行為……

speaker: Unknown
en: ... active influence.
zh: ……主动的影响力。

speaker: Unknown
en: The act of stakeholder management, the act of learning is the 10X skill.
zh: 利益相关者管理的行為，学习的行為，是 10 倍效能的技能。

speaker: Unknown
en: Awesome.
zh: 很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: This is such a fun topic.
zh: 这是一个非常有趣的话题。

speaker: Unknown
en: The way I've been thinking about this is just where are human brains going to continue to be necessary and useful in this new world?
zh: 我思考这个问题的方式仅仅是，人类大脑在这个新世界中将在哪里继续变得有用和必要？

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm terrified.
zh: 我很害怕。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe nowhere.
zh: 也许无处不在。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe nowhere.
zh: 也许无处不在。

speaker: Unknown
en: But the way you described it, which I totally agree with, is essentially it's deciding what to do, and getting everyone on the same page, alignment around that.
zh: 但是你描述它的方式，我完全同意，本质上就是决定做什么，并让每个人达成共识，围绕这一点的一致性。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're all on the same page, here's what we're prioritizing.
zh: 我们都在同一页面上，这就是我们要优先考虑的事情。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then it's like, cool, now let's build it.
zh: 然后就像，酷，现在让我们构建它吧。

speaker: Unknown
en: And that happens so quickly now.
zh: 这现在发生得如此之快。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And what can you notice that AI can't?
zh: 你能注意到什么是 AI 注意不到的？

speaker: Unknown
en: AI is not good at being an anthropologist yet.
zh: AI 还不擅长做人类学家。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is based on a corpus of existing knowledge.
zh: 它是基于现有的知识语料库的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the best product thinkers, again, across functions that I've worked with, really are able to get to novel insights through that user empathy, through that understanding and through deep understanding of the business dynamics, changing industry, and bringing those together to prioritize ideas that actually really, really matter.
zh: 我认为最好的产品思考者，同样，是与我合作过的跨职能的，确实能够通过那种用户同理心、通过那种理解、通过对业务动态、变化的行业的深刻理解，并将这些结合起来来优先考虑那些真正、真正重要的想法，从而获得新颖的见解。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're going to have so much software we won't even know what to do with it, but what software actually works, what software actually matters, what software gets marketed or sold?
zh: 我们将拥有如此多的软件，我们甚至不知道该拿它怎么办，但是什么软件真正有效，什么软件真正重要，什么软件被营销或销售？

speaker: Unknown
en: Or we're going to have to think about not just that the software exists, but that it can get to its final state, and that is going to require people's buy- in to that idea working.
zh: 或者我们将不得不考虑不仅仅是软件存在，而是它可以达到它的最终状态，这将要求人们接受那个想法行得通。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: The way I think about this right now is just where humans are still necessary and great product thinkers are still necessary is on the front end deciding what to prioritize and build, on the back end, determining if this is good and ready, essentially judgment and taste.
zh: 我现在思考这个问题的方式仅仅是，在什么地方人类仍然是必要的，伟大的产品思考者仍然是必要的，是在前端决定要优先考虑和构建什么，在后端，确定这是否是好的和准备好的，本质上是判断力和品味。

speaker: Unknown
en: And then there's distribution I think is such an underappreciated new bottleneck, because as you said, there's so much shit out there.
zh: 然后是分发，我认为这是一个被严重低估的新瓶颈，因为正如你所说，那里有太多的垃圾。

speaker: Unknown
en: Just like, how do we get anyone to pay attention to any of this stuff?
zh: 就像，我们要如何让任何人关注这些东西中的任何一件？

speaker: Unknown
en: Every day, there's a new life-changing product.
zh: 每天都有一个新的改变生活的产品。

speaker: Unknown
en: So I feel like that's going to be a big problem.
zh: 所以我觉得这将是一个大问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, I want you to do a whole episode on distribution because I feel this so much.
zh: 哦，我希望你做一整期关于分发的节目，因为我对此深有感触。

speaker: Unknown
en: If everyone thinks they can build the next sales force on their own, by the way, I don't believe that, then you're going to have just a flood of available tools.
zh: 如果每个人都认为他们可以独自构建下一个 sales force，顺便说一下，我不相信这一点，那么你将会有大量的可用工具泛滥。

speaker: Unknown
en: And who gets the attention?
zh: 谁能获得关注？

speaker: Unknown
en: It is still going to be who has the marketing dollars, who has the brand reputation, who is the existing customer base.
zh: 仍然将是那些拥有营销资金的人，那些拥有品牌声誉的人，那些是现有客户群的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: Distribution is everything.
zh: 分发就是一切。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think the quintessential example we've seen here is with Gemini and Google's tools and just their total continued dominance in that market because of tools of distribution.
zh: 我认为我们在这里看到的一个典型例子是 Gemini 和 Google 的工具，以及他们由于分发工具而在该市场持续的绝对主导地位。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's really fun to see.
zh: 我认为这真的很有趣。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the other piece I would say that's underrated there is trust building, trust with your users, but also with your internal teams.
zh: 我认为我想说的另一件被低估的事情是建立信任，与你的用户的信任，但也与你的内部团队的信任。

speaker: Unknown
en: We're seeing even model companies hire at a huge rate right now.
zh: 我们看到甚至模型公司现在都在以巨大的规模招聘。

speaker: Unknown
en: So you are still working with colleagues.
zh: 所以你仍然是在与同事合作。

speaker: Unknown
en: You are still working with millions of users, and alongside distribution is trust, and that is a function of influence and empathy.
zh: 你仍然在与数百万用户合作，与分发并存的是信任，而这影响力和同理心的函数。

speaker: Unknown
en: Along these lines, what I wonder is, we talked about these other two kind of areas, humans are still valuable and great product leaders are still valuable deciding what to build, knowing if it's ready and awesome.
zh: 沿着这些思路，我想知道的是，我们谈到了这两个其他领域，人类仍然有价值，优秀的产品领导者仍然有价值，决定要构建什么，知道它是否准备好了并且很棒。

speaker: Unknown
en: I wonder how good AI will get at those things because we never thought AI would be amazing at coding and now it's 100% of code will be soon written by AI.
zh: 我想知道 AI 在这些事情上会有多好，因为我们从未想过 AI 会在编码方面表现出色，而现在很快 100% 的代码将由 AI 编写。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I've actually been building a bunch of stuff and I just find Codex and Claude Code are so good at actually giving you ideas for what to build.
zh: 实际上我一直在构建一堆东西，我只是发现 Codex 和 Claude Code 确实非常好，实际上为你提供要构建什么的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm just like, "What should I do to make this better?"
zh: 我就像，“我该怎么做才能让它更好？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like, "Here's 10 ideas."
zh: 它就像，“这里有 10 个想法。”

speaker: Unknown
en: And then they're like, "That is a really good idea."
zh: 然后他们就像，“那真是个好主意。”

speaker: Unknown
en: So I think it'll get there.
zh: 所以我认为它会做到的。

speaker: Unknown
en: People underestimate how good it'll get at these things, I think.
zh: 人们低估了它在这些事情上会变得多好，我认为。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I do think that as ideas proliferate and as there just becomes so many ideas and so much feedback, we're going through a strategy doc rewrite.
zh: 我确实认为，随着想法的激增，随着变得有这么多想法和这么多反馈，我们正在经历策略文档的重写。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think we have like 40 versions now because it's so easy to rev on a 15-page doc.
zh: 我想我们现在有大约 40 个版本，因为在 15 页的文档上迭代非常容易。

speaker: Unknown
en: Actually, when these decisions are happening faster, building is happening faster, the flip side of that is mistakes compound faster as well.
zh: 实际上，当这些决策发生得更快，构建发生得更快时，反过来说是错误也复合得更快。

speaker: Unknown
en: And strategy clarity becomes so much more important to anchor people on.
zh: 策略清晰度变得如此重要，以此作为人们的锚点。

speaker: Unknown
en: If you want to have an empowered EPDI organization or company, that clarity of what you believe, what are the most important problems to invest time and energy into, where we want to invest compute and dollars, that is the mechanism for letting people go and run at as fast a velocity as they're capable of.
zh: 如果你想拥有一个赋能的 EPDI 组织或公司，这种清晰度——你相信什么，投资时间和精力的最重要问题是什么，我们要在哪里投资算力和美元——就是让人们尽可能以他们能达到的速度去奔跑的机制。

speaker: Unknown
en: So if you have that corpus of shared beliefs that you update on a pretty fast clip, because I think that's the mode we're in, that is actually what enables teams to build the right stuff as opposed to just the idea that Claude had.
zh: 所以如果你拥有那套共享的信念库，并且你以相当快的速度更新它，因为我认为这就是我们所处的模式，这实际上就是使团队能够构建正确的东西，而不仅仅是 Claude 的想法的机制。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love this point because there's a lot of talk on Twitter actually just today in the PM community about PRDs are dead.
zh: 我喜欢这一点，因为实际上就在今天，在 PM 社区的 Twitter 上有很多关于 PRD 已死的讨论。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think it's exactly the opposite, which is being very clear about what we are doing and the strategy is now the most important thing, because once you have that, then it's cool.
zh: 我认为恰恰相反，也就是非常清楚我们在做什么以及策略现在是最重要的事情，因为一旦你有了那个，那就很酷了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Fire off a hundred agents and we'll go build it and launch it.
zh: 发射一百个智能体，我们将去构建它并发布它。

speaker: Unknown
en: Totally.
zh: 完全正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: Strategy clarity is so, so important.
zh: 策略清晰度是如此、如此重要。

speaker: Unknown
en: I am not in the Twitter verse.
zh: 我不在 Twitter 界里。

speaker: Unknown
en: I find it exhausting, partially because I think takes like PRDs are dead or software is dead is like really dumb.
zh: 我觉得它让人筋疲力尽，部分是因为我认为像 PRD 已死或软件已死这样的观点真的很愚蠢。

speaker: Unknown
en: I find it a little insufferable if I'm being spicy, because I still think what matters is understanding users, understanding business and economic dynamics, understanding strategy and what we're trying to accomplish, and then really thinking through what is the right way to do that together.
zh: 如果我要激进一点的话，我觉得这有点难以忍受，因为我仍然认为重要的是理解用户，理解商业和经济动态，理解策略和我们试图完成什么，然后真正深入思考一起做这件事的正确方式是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Maybe one more question along these lines, just agents now running as colleagues.
zh: 也许沿着这些思路再问一个问题，就是智能体现在作为同事运行。

speaker: Unknown
en: Everyone's got all these Claudes running, Codexes, all kinds of agent platforms.
zh: 每个人都有所有这些 Claude 在运行，Codexes，各种智能体平台。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now that agents are a part of the workforce and increasingly, is there anything there around influence that is valuable for people to start thinking about to learn how to do?
zh: 既然智能体是劳动力的一部分并且越来越多，关于影响力是否有任何值得人们开始思考去学习如何做的事情？

speaker: Unknown
en: I love that question.
zh: 我喜欢这个问题。

speaker: Unknown
en: We talk about this a lot, especially when we think about identity, security, compliance, that agents are a teammate for accelerating work, and the risks that that also provides because of the places where agents fall down.
zh: 我们经常谈论这一点，特别是当我们考虑身份、安全、合规性时，即智能体是加速工作的队友，以及这也带来的风险，因为智能体表现不佳的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think agents are absolutely something we have to think about with relation to influence, because we are basically all directors of work now, right?
zh: 我认为智能体绝对是我们必须考虑与影响力有关的事情，因为我们基本上现在都是工作的主管，对吧？

speaker: Unknown
en: If we have an army of agents or this hundred new colleagues that joined our team, how would we onboard new junior team members who don't already understand our product philosophy, what's important to us, and how do we codify that for ourselves?
zh: 如果我们有一支智能体军队或者这 100 位加入我们团队的新同事，我们要如何入职那些还不了解我们的产品理念、什么对我们重要的初级团队成员，我们要如何为自己将其编纂成文？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do we take the time to say, "What's important to me as a product leader that I want to inculcate into anything that's developed for my team?
zh: 我们如何花时间说，“作为产品领导者，什么对我很重要，我想灌输到为我的团队开发的任何东西中？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do I believe about product market fit?
zh: 我对产品市场契合度有什么信念？

speaker: Unknown
en: What do I look for in terms of what success looks like?
zh: 就成功看起来是什么样的，我寻求什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: How do I set metrics?"
zh: 我如何设定指标？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And if we can do that upfront work to actually analyze ourselves and where we've seen success, where we've seen downfalls, and then continue to train our agent models with that data, that history, that context, they will be better off.
zh: 如果我们能做这些前期工作来实际分析我们自己，以及我们在哪里看到成功，在哪里看到失败，然后用这些数据、历史、背景继续训练我们的智能体模型，它们会更好。

speaker: Unknown
en: But I also think the flip side of this is guardrails.
zh: 但我也认为这方面的反面是护栏。

speaker: Unknown
en: Where do you need to be involved?
zh: 在哪里你需要参与？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where do you need to catch hallucinations or missteps?
zh: 在哪里你需要捕捉幻觉或失误？

speaker: Unknown
en: Where do you uniquely have taste, judgment, frame of reference, something intangible where you need to say, "Don't do this without me."
zh: 在哪里你独特地拥有品味、判断、参考框架、某种无形的东西，你需要说，“没有我不要做这个。”

speaker: Unknown
en: What's so funny about this, essentially we're helping the agent learn the skills we've been talking about to influence you.
zh: 关于这最有趣的是，本质上我们正在帮助智能体学习我们一直在谈论的影响你的技能。

speaker: Unknown
en: Here's context on my goals, here's what I understand about the customers, here's our current priorities.
zh: 这是关于我的目标的背景，这是我对客户的理解，这些是我们当前的优先级。

speaker: Unknown
en: Help me.
zh: 帮帮我。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tell me what to do.
zh: 告诉我该做什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: And it's like influencing you to agree.
zh: 这就像在影响你以表示同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: Absolutely.
zh: 绝对正确。

speaker: Unknown
en: Wow, that's really the singularity, isn't it?
zh: 哇，这真的是奇点，不是吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: You should just feed this podcast to a bunch of agents and see if they can do this better than me, see what their advice is.
zh: 你应该直接把这期播客喂给一群智能体，看看它们能不能比我做得更好，看看它们的建议是什么。

speaker: Unknown
en: No, but I really think at this point we are in a place where it is a back and forth and where we should be using agents and AI as a really smart colleague that never gets irritated by our questions.
zh: 不，但我真的认为在这一点上，我们处于一个来回往复的阶段，我们应该把智能体和 AI 作为一个真正聪明的同事使用，它永远不会对我们的问题感到厌烦。

speaker: Unknown
en: Poke holes in my ideas.
zh: 挑刺我的想法。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tell me what you would do differently.
zh: 告诉我你会怎么做。

speaker: Unknown
en: Help me plan this out.
zh: 帮我规划这件事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Base this on more corporate context than I could ever have gone through myself and poke holes.
zh: 基于比我自己能处理的更多的企业背景来做这些，并挑刺。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think that that is just a really powerful way for amplifying our work.
zh: 我认为这确实是放大我们工作的一种非常强大的方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's so interesting.
zh: 这太有趣了。

speaker: Unknown
en: When your tips has come into an open mind and learn, it's like exactly what an agent does.
zh: 当你的提示以开放的心态进入并学习时，这就像是一个智能体所做的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Tell me more, what else should I do?
zh: 多告诉我一些，我还应该做什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: And then just hopping on your ideas like, "Okay, cool.
zh: 然后就像抓住你的想法一样，“好的，很酷。

speaker: Unknown
en: We'll add that to the..." All the ideas you've shared, agents are so good at.
zh: 我们把它加到……”你分享的所有想法，智能体都非常擅长。

speaker: Unknown
en: So that's the world-
zh: 所以这就是这个世界——

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that one thing I'm really curious about, you were talking about human brain chemistry, is just how we can protect our brains from overwhelm.
zh: 我真的很好奇的一件事是，你谈到人类的大脑化学，就是我们如何保护我们的大脑免于不知所措。

speaker: Unknown
en: The speed of ideas, change, building is... We are dis-evolved for this pace of life.
zh: 想法的速度、变化、建设的速度……我们对这种生活节奏是逆向进化的。

speaker: Unknown
en: We were evolved... I love this book, Pachinko, that tells the history of a Korean family over several generations.
zh: 我们进化了……我喜欢这本书，《Pachinko》，它讲述了一个韩国家庭几代人的历史。

speaker: Unknown
en: And in one of the generations sort of in the 1600s, 1700s, there are two wives that every day cook breakfast then go to the market, buy rice, buy fish, buy vegetables, go home, cook the rice, cook the fish, serve dinner, clean up dinner.
zh: 在其中一代人中，大概是在 1600 年代、1700 年代，有两个妻子，每天做早饭，然后去市场，买大米，买鱼，买蔬菜，回家，煮米饭，煮鱼，端上晚餐，清理晚餐。

speaker: Unknown
en: That's the day.
zh: 这就是一天。

speaker: Unknown
en: And for the vast majority of human history, that was kind of our day.
zh: 在人类历史的绝大部分时间里，这就是我们要过的一天。

speaker: Unknown
en: And the mental load that we are asking of our brains right now is staggering.
zh: 我们现在要求大脑承担的精神负荷是令人震惊的。

speaker: Unknown
en: And so I think one of the ways that we actually have to use AI and agents is to help our brains be most effective, is to clear out distraction, to point out the most important things, to... And I think this is where people really need to make some tough decisions.
zh: 所以我认为我们必须使用 AI 和智能体的方式之一是帮助我们的大脑最有效，是清除干扰，指出最重要的事情，……我认为这是人们真正需要做出一些艰难决定的地方。

speaker: Unknown
en: How much can we really focus on and how do we allow for that focus in the ways that are hopefully good for humanity?
zh: 我们真正能专注于多少，以及我们如何以希望对人类有益的方式允许这种专注？

speaker: Unknown
en: What a time to be alive, Jessica.
zh: 这是一个活在当下的好时代，Jessica。

speaker: Unknown
en: Holy... Oh my God.
zh: 天哪……哦我的上帝。

speaker: Unknown
en: Before we get to our very exciting lightning ground, is there anything else that you wanted to share?
zh: 在我们进入非常令人兴奋的闪电轮之前，你还有什么想分享的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: Anything else you want to double down on?
zh: 还有什么你想加倍强调的吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: One thing I think that I'll say is I'm a people person.
zh: 我想我要说的一件事是我是一个以人为本的人。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love in-person collaboration and I love talking ideas through and whiteboard sessions.
zh: 我喜欢面对面协作，我喜欢讨论想法和白板会议。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm a two wing three.
zh: 我是两翼第三型。

speaker: Unknown
en: If there are any other Enneagram fans out there, it's a mix of the people person, the sort of bringing the communal thinker with the achiever.
zh: 如果还有其他九型人格粉丝的话，这是以人为本的人、将 communal thinker 与实干者结合的混合体。

speaker: Unknown
en: That is my personality type.
zh: 这是我的性格类型。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that sometimes I can give this kind of advice and people say, "Oh, well, Jess, you're good with people," or, "You kind of understand these things."
zh: 我认为有时我可以给出这种建议，人们会说，“噢，好吧，Jess，你很擅长与人打交道，”或者，“你理解这些事情。”

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that something that has taken me a while in my career is how to be authentic to myself in influence, in relationships, in trust building.
zh: 我认为我在职业生涯中花了很长时间的一件事是如何在影响力、人际关系、建立信任方面对自己保持真实。

speaker: Unknown
en: And I don't think that it's one personality type or another.
zh: 我认为这不是某一种性格类型或另一种性格类型。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that you could be shy and introverted or super data-oriented and technical.
zh: 我认为你可以害羞内向，或者超级数据导向和技术型。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think all types of personalities can, in their own authentic way, communicate with the people around them to build trust, to build influence in the way that feels true to them.
zh: 我认为所有类型的性格都可以以他们自己真实的方式与周围的人交流，以建立信任，以建立影响力，这种方式对他们来说是真实的。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think when I was younger in my career and I was starting out as a PM, I got a lot of feedback of, "Oh, Jess, how do you show up more like a man would, show up more aggressively, show up more decisively?"
zh: 我认为当我职业生涯刚开始担任 PM 时，我得到了很多反馈，比如，“噢，Jess，你怎么表现得更像男人一点，表现得更激进一点，表现得更果断一点？”

speaker: Unknown
en: And I think there were some good nuggets in there, but I think it took me a while to feel like, hey, who I am and how I show up is actually my superpower that I can double down on, and I don't need to be like someone else.
zh: 我认为那里有一些好的建议，但我认为我花了一段时间才感觉到，嘿，我是谁以及我如何表现实际上是我的超能力，我可以加倍利用它，我不需要像别人一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: What I need to do is think about what I want to accomplish and how I want to grow and do that in a way that is true to me.
zh: 我需要做的是思考我想要完成什么，以及我想要如何成长，并以对我真实的方式做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: I could not agree more with that.
zh: 我非常同意这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've shared very similar sentiment on the pod a few times, just like you can accomplish the same things other people can through your own strengths.
zh: 我在播客上多次分享过非常相似的情绪，就像你可以通过自己的优势完成其他人能完成的事情一样。

speaker: Unknown
en: You don't have to do it the way they're doing it.
zh: 你不必用他们的方式去做。

speaker: Jessica Fain
en: Exactly.
zh: 确实如此。

speaker: Unknown
en: I so agree.
zh: 我非常同意。

speaker: Unknown
en: A beautiful way to end that.
zh: 这是一个结束这一部分的完美方式。

speaker: Unknown
en: And with that, we have reached our very exciting lightning round.
zh: 有了这个，我们已经到了我们非常令人兴奋的闪电轮。

speaker: Unknown
en: I've got five questions for you.
zh: 我有五个问题要问你。

en: Are you ready?
zh: 你准备好了吗？

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm ready.
zh: 我准备好了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I'm so excited.
zh: 我太兴奋了。

speaker: Unknown
en: What are two or three books that you find yourself recommending most to other people?
zh: 你发现你自己最常推荐给别人的两三本书是什么？

speaker: Unknown
en: I think there's a genre of book I've really fallen in love with.
zh: 我想有一个类别的书我真的很喜欢。

speaker: Unknown
en: I mentioned Pachinko before.
zh: 我之前提到了《柏青哥》。

speaker: Unknown
en: I love historical fiction because it really gives me access into worlds that I don't know.
zh: 我喜欢历史小说，因为它真的让我进入我不了解的世界。

speaker: Unknown
en: I can't remember who talks about windows and mirrors.
zh: 我不记得是谁谈论过窗户和镜子。

speaker: Unknown
en: Books are windows or mirrors for us.
zh: 书对我们来说是窗户或镜子。

speaker: Unknown
en: And historical fiction, especially about places where I'm less familiar with that history, is really a window into someone else's experience.
zh: 历史小说，尤其是关于我不太熟悉其历史的地方，真的是了解他人体验的一扇窗户。

speaker: Unknown
en: And there's a particular type of historical fiction that I've just loved, which is multi-generational historical fiction.
zh: 有一种特定类型的历史小说我刚刚很喜欢，那就是多代人的历史小说。

speaker: Unknown
en: Pachinko does this with a family in Korea over generations and moving to Japan.
zh: 《柏青哥》通过一个家庭在韩国几代人的经历以及搬到日本来做到这一点。

speaker: Unknown
en: There's a book called Homegoing, which is about the West Coast of Africa and the impact of the slave trade and the ongoing life in Africa from two split parts of the family.
zh: 有一本书叫《归乡》，是关于非洲西海岸和奴隶贸易的影响，以及家族分裂的两部分在非洲的后续生活。

speaker: Unknown
en: History of Burning is a book that tells a story of Indian indentured servants that were brought to Uganda to build the British railroad there and just parts of history that I was so much less familiar with and really treasure that sort of window into a different world.
zh: 《燃烧的历史》是一本讲述印度契约仆人的故事，他们被带到乌干达修建那里的英国铁路，这部分历史我非常不熟悉，但我真的很珍视这种通往不同世界的窗口。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think the book Overstory is an example of that.
zh: 我想《树冠》就是这方面的一个例子。

en: Have you heard of that?
zh: 你听说过那个吗？

en: No, I haven't.
zh: 不，我没有。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think.
zh: 我想。

en: I might be getting it wrong, but it's called Overstory, which is a concept of when you have a big tree, like the overstory is the branches that kind of sit on top, that kind of create a shade.
zh: 我可能搞错了，但它叫《树冠》，这是一个概念，当你有一棵大树时，就像树冠是位于顶部的树枝，那种创造树荫的。

speaker: Jessica Fain
en: Oh, that's so beautiful.
zh: 哦，那太美了。

speaker: Unknown
en: I think that's the book I'm thinking about, and it's the story of a family over many generations and around a tree.
zh: 我想那就是我想到的那本书，它讲述的是一个家庭跨越几代人围绕着一棵树的故事。

speaker: Unknown
en: Oh, great.
zh: 哦，太棒了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Now I have to pick that up.
zh: 现在我必须去读那本书了。

speaker: Unknown
en: Yeah.
zh: 是的。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's popular.
zh: 它很受欢迎。

en: Okay.
zh: 好的。

speaker: Unknown
en: If it's the one I'm thinking about, I think you're going to love it.
zh: 如果是我想的那本，我想你会喜欢的。

speaker: Unknown
en: Okay, great.
zh: 好的，太好了。

en: I'll [inaudible 01:26:48].

en: Favorite recent movie or TV show you've really enjoyed?
zh: 你最近真的很喜欢的最喜欢的电影或电视节目？

en: Again, this is another window.
zh: 同样，这是另一个窗口。

speaker: Unknown
en: I have been obsessively watching The Pitt, which is probably a lame answer.
zh: 我一直在着迷地看《匹特》，这可能是一个无聊的答案。

en: I wonder if anyone else has given you that so far, but it's given me such a vantage point and a view into the incredibly difficult work that emergency healthcare workers do and the stresses and the system and the brokenness that they operate under, but also the extreme care and love that they bring to their work and has just given me so much admiration.
zh: 我想知道到目前为止是否还有其他人给过你这个答案，但它给了我这样一个有利的地位和视角，去了解急诊医疗工作者所做的极其困难的工作，以及他们所承受的压力和系统的破碎，但他们也带入了极大的关怀和爱，这让我产生了如此多的钦佩。

speaker: Unknown
en: It is trending on this podcast and I'm watching it as well.
zh: 它在这个播客上很流行，我也在看。

speaker: Unknown
en: It's really good.
zh: 它真的很好。

en: It is really good.

en: Okay.
